entries.scenario: The controller at a wholesale products distributor needs the official monthly revenue figure that ties to the income statement, sourced from posted GL activity rather than transaction headers.
entries.question: What is our GL-posted revenue by accounting period (month) for a given date range?
entries.intent: Sums posted transactionaccountingline amounts on Income-type accounts per non-adjustment month period, sign-corrected so credits (revenue) are positive.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    ap.periodname AS period_name,
    TO_CHAR(ap.startdate, 'YYYY-MM') AS period_month,
    SUM(-tal.amount * t.exchangerate) AS revenue_base
FROM transactionaccountingline tal
JOIN transaction t ON t.id = tal.transaction
JOIN account a ON a.id = tal.account
JOIN accountingperiod ap ON ap.id = t.postingperiod
WHERE tal.posting = 'T'
    AND a.accttype = 'Income'
    AND ap.isadjust = 'F'
    AND ap.isquarter = 'F'
    AND ap.isyear = 'F'
    AND ap.startdate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND ap.startdate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY ap.periodname, TO_CHAR(ap.startdate, 'YYYY-MM')
ORDER BY TO_CHAR(ap.startdate, 'YYYY-MM')
entries.caveats: GL truth requires transactionaccountingline with posting='T'; income = credit - debit, and amount is debit-signed, so revenue is SUM(-amount). accountingperiod contains quarter/year rollup rows, so isadjust='F' AND isquarter='F' AND isyear='F' are mandatory when listing months. Period join goes through transaction.postingperiod, not trandate. exchangerate converts to the transaction subsidiary's base; cross-subsidiary totals assume a common base currency — add BUILTIN.DF(tl.subsidiary) grouping if bases differ. Add 'OthIncome' to the accttype filter if non-operating income should be included.

entries.scenario: A merchandising manager at a retail products company wants to see which SKUs actually drive revenue so she can prioritize reorder and promotion decisions.
entries.question: Which products generated the most revenue (and how many units) over a date range?
entries.intent: Ranks real product items by line-level net revenue and units sold across invoices and cash sales, sign-corrected and excluding pseudo-items.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    i.itemid AS item_sku,
    i.displayname AS item_name,
    i.itemtype AS item_type,
    SUM(-tl.quantity) AS units_sold,
    SUM(-tl.netamount * t.exchangerate) AS revenue_base
FROM transactionline tl
JOIN transaction t ON t.id = tl.transaction
JOIN item i ON i.id = tl.item
WHERE t.type IN ('CustInvc', 'CashSale')
    AND tl.mainline = 'F'
    AND tl.taxline = 'F'
    AND i.itemtype IN ('InvtPart', 'NonInvtPart', 'Assembly', 'Kit', 'Service', 'OthCharge', 'DwnLdItem', 'GiftCert')
    AND t.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND t.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY i.itemid, i.displayname, i.itemtype
ORDER BY SUM(-tl.netamount * t.exchangerate) DESC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: Sales line netamount and quantity are NEGATIVE in transactionline, so both are negated. mainline='F' AND taxline='F' prevents double-counting header/tax rows. itemtype is constrained to real sellable items so Subtotal/Markup/Discount pseudo-items don't distort per-item sums (this also means header-level discount lines are excluded from item revenue). exchangerate converts each line to its subsidiary base — consolidated ranking assumes a common base currency. Credit memos are not netted here (see FIN-REV-009).

entries.scenario: An account management lead at a B2B wholesale company needs a top-customers list for quarterly business reviews and to spot revenue concentration risk.
entries.question: Who are our top N customers by invoiced revenue in a date range?
entries.intent: Ranks customers by total header invoice amount (base-currency converted) with invoice counts, using the customer join for AR entity resolution.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    c.entityid AS customer_id,
    c.companyname AS customer_name,
    COUNT(t.id) AS invoice_count,
    SUM(t.foreigntotal * t.exchangerate) AS invoiced_revenue_base
FROM transaction t
JOIN customer c ON c.id = t.entity
WHERE t.type = 'CustInvc'
    AND t.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND t.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY c.entityid, c.companyname
ORDER BY SUM(t.foreigntotal * t.exchangerate) DESC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: entity is polymorphic — CustInvc is an AR type, so the join target must be customer (a vendor join would silently drop rows). Header foreigntotal on invoices is positive and GROSS (includes tax and shipping); for net product revenue use the line grain (FIN-REV-002). Credit memos are not netted here. exchangerate converts to each transaction's subsidiary base; cross-subsidiary ranking assumes one base currency. No status filter is used — never filter status='open'; use foreignamountunpaid if only unpaid invoices are wanted.

entries.scenario: The FP&A analyst at a multi-subsidiary consumer goods distributor builds a quarterly revenue trend per subsidiary for the board deck.
entries.question: What is our net line-level sales revenue by quarter, broken down by subsidiary?
entries.intent: Sums sign-corrected invoice and cash-sale line revenue per calendar quarter and subsidiary (subsidiary resolved from transactionline).
entries.sql:
SELECT
    TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY') || '-Q' || TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'Q') AS sales_quarter,
    BUILTIN.DF(tl.subsidiary) AS subsidiary_name,
    SUM(-tl.netamount * t.exchangerate) AS revenue_base
FROM transactionline tl
JOIN transaction t ON t.id = tl.transaction
WHERE t.type IN ('CustInvc', 'CashSale')
    AND tl.mainline = 'F'
    AND tl.taxline = 'F'
    AND t.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND t.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY') || '-Q' || TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'Q'), BUILTIN.DF(tl.subsidiary)
ORDER BY TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY') || '-Q' || TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'Q'), BUILTIN.DF(tl.subsidiary)
entries.caveats: transaction.subsidiary is NOT queryable (NOT_EXPOSED) — subsidiary comes from transactionline with BUILTIN.DF. Sales line netamount is negative, hence SUM(-netamount); mainline='F' AND taxline='F' avoids header/tax double counting. Full GROUP BY expressions are repeated (no aliases allowed in GROUP BY). exchangerate converts each line to its own subsidiary base, so each subsidiary row is in that subsidiary's base currency — do not sum rows across subsidiaries with different base currencies. Calendar quarters from trandate, not fiscal periods; use accountingperiod (FIN-REV-001) for fiscal alignment.

entries.scenario: A sales director at a products wholesaler runs monthly commission and performance reviews and needs each rep's invoiced revenue and deal count.
entries.question: How much revenue did each sales rep generate from invoices in a date range, ranked highest first?
entries.intent: Ranks sales reps (transaction.employee) by sign-corrected invoice line revenue with distinct invoice counts.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    e.entityid AS sales_rep,
    COUNT(DISTINCT t.id) AS invoice_count,
    SUM(-tl.netamount * t.exchangerate) AS revenue_base
FROM transactionline tl
JOIN transaction t ON t.id = tl.transaction
JOIN employee e ON e.id = t.employee
WHERE t.type = 'CustInvc'
    AND tl.mainline = 'F'
    AND tl.taxline = 'F'
    AND t.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND t.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY e.entityid
ORDER BY SUM(-tl.netamount * t.exchangerate) DESC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: transaction.employee holds the sales rep on sales transactions; the INNER JOIN silently drops invoices with no rep assigned — switch to a LEFT JOIN with NVL if an 'Unassigned' bucket is needed. Line netamount is negative on sales, hence SUM(-netamount) with mainline='F' AND taxline='F'. COUNT(DISTINCT t.id) is required because the line grain repeats the header per line. Credit memos are not netted, so refunded deals still credit the rep here.

entries.scenario: An e-commerce operations manager at a retail products company tracks average order value monthly to measure the impact of bundling and free-shipping threshold changes.
entries.question: What is our monthly sales order count, total booked value, and average order value?
entries.intent: Computes per-month sales order count, summed header value, and AOV from sales order headers.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM') AS order_month,
    COUNT(t.id) AS order_count,
    SUM(t.foreigntotal * t.exchangerate) AS total_booked_base,
    ROUND(SUM(t.foreigntotal * t.exchangerate) / COUNT(t.id), 2) AS avg_order_value_base
FROM transaction t
WHERE t.type = 'SalesOrd'
    AND t.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND t.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM')
ORDER BY TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM')
entries.caveats: Sales orders are non-posting, so this is BOOKED demand, not recognized revenue (use FIN-REV-001 for GL revenue). Header foreigntotal on sales orders is positive and gross of tax/shipping. Header grain (no transactionline) means no mainline filter is needed, avoiding line double-counting entirely. No status filter — cancelled/closed orders are included; status is a raw letter code, so display it with BUILTIN.DF(t.status) if you need to segment, never filter status='open'.

entries.scenario: A billing team lead at a wholesale distributor monitors invoicing throughput and active-customer breadth to catch month-end billing backlogs early.
entries.question: How many invoices did we issue each month, and how many distinct customers were billed?
entries.intent: Counts invoices and distinct billed customers per calendar month from transaction headers.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM') AS invoice_month,
    COUNT(t.id) AS invoice_count,
    COUNT(DISTINCT t.entity) AS customers_billed
FROM transaction t
WHERE t.type = 'CustInvc'
    AND t.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND t.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM')
ORDER BY TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM')
entries.caveats: Pure header grain — counting at the transactionline grain would inflate counts per line, so no line join is used. t.entity is counted directly without joining customer (the join is unnecessary for a distinct count and would be to customer, not vendor, since CustInvc is an AR type). Months come from trandate; switch to the accountingperiod join via t.postingperiod if fiscal-period alignment matters.

entries.scenario: The VP of Sales at a products company reviews which accounts are growing or shrinking year over year, comparing YTD revenue against the same window last year to plan retention outreach.
entries.question: For each customer, what is YTD revenue versus the same period last year, and what is the change?
entries.intent: Pivots sign-corrected invoice/cash-sale line revenue into current-YTD and prior-year-same-window CASE buckets per customer with the delta, ranked by current YTD.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    c.entityid AS customer_id,
    c.companyname AS customer_name,
    SUM(CASE WHEN t.trandate >= TRUNC(SYSDATE, 'YEAR')
        THEN -tl.netamount * t.exchangerate ELSE 0 END) AS ytd_revenue,
    SUM(CASE WHEN t.trandate >= ADD_MONTHS(TRUNC(SYSDATE, 'YEAR'), -12)
             AND t.trandate <= ADD_MONTHS(TRUNC(SYSDATE), -12)
        THEN -tl.netamount * t.exchangerate ELSE 0 END) AS prior_ytd_revenue,
    SUM(CASE WHEN t.trandate >= TRUNC(SYSDATE, 'YEAR')
        THEN -tl.netamount * t.exchangerate ELSE 0 END)
    - SUM(CASE WHEN t.trandate >= ADD_MONTHS(TRUNC(SYSDATE, 'YEAR'), -12)
               AND t.trandate <= ADD_MONTHS(TRUNC(SYSDATE), -12)
          THEN -tl.netamount * t.exchangerate ELSE 0 END) AS revenue_change
FROM transactionline tl
JOIN transaction t ON t.id = tl.transaction
JOIN customer c ON c.id = t.entity
WHERE t.type IN ('CustInvc', 'CashSale')
    AND tl.mainline = 'F'
    AND tl.taxline = 'F'
    AND t.trandate >= ADD_MONTHS(TRUNC(SYSDATE, 'YEAR'), -12)
    AND t.trandate <= TRUNC(SYSDATE)
GROUP BY c.entityid, c.companyname
HAVING SUM(CASE WHEN t.trandate >= TRUNC(SYSDATE, 'YEAR')
        THEN -tl.netamount * t.exchangerate ELSE 0 END) <> 0
    OR SUM(CASE WHEN t.trandate >= ADD_MONTHS(TRUNC(SYSDATE, 'YEAR'), -12)
             AND t.trandate <= ADD_MONTHS(TRUNC(SYSDATE), -12)
        THEN -tl.netamount * t.exchangerate ELSE 0 END) <> 0
ORDER BY SUM(CASE WHEN t.trandate >= TRUNC(SYSDATE, 'YEAR')
        THEN -tl.netamount * t.exchangerate ELSE 0 END) DESC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: CASE buckets make the comparison apples-to-apples: prior bucket ends at ADD_MONTHS(TRUNC(SYSDATE), -12), the same day-of-year as today, so a full prior year is NOT compared against a partial current year. The WHERE window spans prior-Jan-1 to today, so prior-year rows dated after today's anniversary fall into neither bucket (ELSE 0) by design; the HAVING clause drops customers who are zero in both buckets. Expressions are repeated verbatim in HAVING/ORDER BY (no aliases). Sales lines negated with mainline='F'/taxline='F'; customer join because CustInvc/CashSale are AR types. Credit memos are excluded — combine with FIN-REV-009 logic for net-of-returns growth.

entries.scenario: The revenue accountant at a consumer products wholesaler reports net sales after returns each month and must show gross billings, credit-memo offsets, and the net figure that reconciles to the P&L direction.
entries.question: What is our monthly gross revenue, credit memo total, and net revenue after credit memos?
entries.intent: Nets invoice and cash-sale line revenue against credit-memo lines per month, exposing gross, credits, and net columns via type-based CASE buckets.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM') AS sales_month,
    SUM(CASE WHEN t.type IN ('CustInvc', 'CashSale')
        THEN -tl.netamount * t.exchangerate ELSE 0 END) AS gross_revenue,
    SUM(CASE WHEN t.type = 'CustCred'
        THEN tl.netamount * t.exchangerate ELSE 0 END) AS credit_memo_total,
    SUM(-tl.netamount * t.exchangerate) AS net_revenue
FROM transactionline tl
JOIN transaction t ON t.id = tl.transaction
WHERE t.type IN ('CustInvc', 'CashSale', 'CustCred')
    AND tl.mainline = 'F'
    AND tl.taxline = 'F'
    AND t.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND t.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM')
ORDER BY TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM')
entries.caveats: Sign symmetry does the netting: invoice/cash-sale lines carry NEGATIVE netamount while credit-memo (CustCred) lines carry POSITIVE netamount, so SUM(-netamount) across all three types yields net revenue automatically; the CASE columns merely display gross and credits (credits shown as a positive offset) separately. mainline='F' AND taxline='F' is essential on every bucket. This is transactional net sales by trandate month — it approximates but does not replace GL revenue (FIN-REV-001), which also captures journal entries and posting-period differences. exchangerate converts to each subsidiary's base; consolidation assumes one base currency.

entries.scenario: The AR clerk at a wholesale distributor starts each morning by pulling every invoice that still has an open balance so collections work can be prioritized.
entries.question: Show me all open customer invoices with their outstanding balances and due dates.
entries.intent: Lists every CustInvc header with a non-zero foreignamountunpaid, showing customer, dates, display status, and open amount in transaction currency.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    t.tranid AS invoice_number,
    c.entityid AS customer,
    t.trandate AS invoice_date,
    t.duedate AS due_date,
    BUILTIN.DF(t.status) AS status,
    t.foreignamountunpaid AS open_amount,
    BUILTIN.DF(t.currency) AS currency
FROM transaction t
JOIN customer c ON c.id = t.entity
WHERE t.type = 'CustInvc'
    AND t.foreignamountunpaid <> 0
ORDER BY t.duedate, c.entityid
entries.caveats: Openness is filtered via foreignamountunpaid <> 0, never status='open' (status is a raw per-type letter code); BUILTIN.DF(t.status) is used only for display. foreigntotal is NOT used for the balance because it ignores partial payments. Amounts are in each invoice's transaction currency — multiply by t.exchangerate for base-currency totals.

entries.scenario: The controller of a consumer-products wholesaler reviews a per-customer AR aging every week to decide which accounts to put on credit hold.
entries.question: Give me an AR aging report by customer with current, 1-30, 31-60, 61-90, and over-90-day buckets.
entries.intent: Buckets open CustInvc foreignamountunpaid (converted to base via exchangerate) by days past due date per customer using conditional aggregation.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    c.entityid AS customer,
    SUM(CASE WHEN NVL(t.duedate, t.trandate) >= TRUNC(SYSDATE)
        THEN t.foreignamountunpaid * t.exchangerate ELSE 0 END) AS current_amt,
    SUM(CASE WHEN NVL(t.duedate, t.trandate) < TRUNC(SYSDATE)
            AND NVL(t.duedate, t.trandate) >= TRUNC(SYSDATE) - 30
        THEN t.foreignamountunpaid * t.exchangerate ELSE 0 END) AS days_1_30,
    SUM(CASE WHEN NVL(t.duedate, t.trandate) < TRUNC(SYSDATE) - 30
            AND NVL(t.duedate, t.trandate) >= TRUNC(SYSDATE) - 60
        THEN t.foreignamountunpaid * t.exchangerate ELSE 0 END) AS days_31_60,
    SUM(CASE WHEN NVL(t.duedate, t.trandate) < TRUNC(SYSDATE) - 60
            AND NVL(t.duedate, t.trandate) >= TRUNC(SYSDATE) - 90
        THEN t.foreignamountunpaid * t.exchangerate ELSE 0 END) AS days_61_90,
    SUM(CASE WHEN NVL(t.duedate, t.trandate) < TRUNC(SYSDATE) - 90
        THEN t.foreignamountunpaid * t.exchangerate ELSE 0 END) AS days_over_90,
    SUM(t.foreignamountunpaid * t.exchangerate) AS total_open
FROM transaction t
JOIN customer c ON c.id = t.entity
WHERE t.type = 'CustInvc'
    AND t.foreignamountunpaid <> 0
GROUP BY c.entityid
ORDER BY SUM(t.foreignamountunpaid * t.exchangerate) DESC
entries.caveats: Open balance comes from foreignamountunpaid (not foreigntotal, not status filtering). duedate can be NULL (no-terms invoices), so NVL(duedate, trandate) prevents rows from vanishing out of every bucket. Amounts are converted with t.exchangerate to each transaction's subsidiary base currency; cross-subsidiary consolidation assumes bases are comparable — group by BUILTIN.DF(tl.subsidiary) via transactionline if you need per-subsidiary splits. GROUP BY repeats the raw expression, not the alias.

entries.scenario: The CFO of a mid-market distribution company tracks Days Sales Outstanding each month to gauge whether collections are keeping pace with revenue growth.
entries.question: What is our current DSO based on open receivables and the last 91 days of invoiced sales?
entries.intent: Single-scan conditional aggregation over CustInvc headers computing open AR in base currency, trailing 91-day invoiced sales in base currency, and DSO = open_ar / sales * 91.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    SUM(t.foreignamountunpaid * t.exchangerate) AS open_ar_base,
    SUM(CASE WHEN t.trandate >= TRUNC(SYSDATE) - 91
        THEN t.foreigntotal * t.exchangerate ELSE 0 END) AS invoiced_sales_91d_base,
    ROUND(
        SUM(t.foreignamountunpaid * t.exchangerate)
        / NULLIF(SUM(CASE WHEN t.trandate >= TRUNC(SYSDATE) - 91
            THEN t.foreigntotal * t.exchangerate ELSE 0 END), 0)
        * 91, 1) AS dso_days
FROM transaction t
WHERE t.type = 'CustInvc'
entries.caveats: This is the simple-average DSO approximation (open AR / trailing-91-day invoiced sales * 91), not the countback method. Numerator uses foreignamountunpaid across ALL invoices regardless of age; denominator uses foreigntotal only for invoices dated in the window. Credit memos and unapplied payments are deliberately excluded — including CustCred would require negating its credit-signed foreigntotal. exchangerate converts each invoice to its own subsidiary's base; consolidating across subsidiaries assumes a common base currency. NULLIF guards divide-by-zero when there are no recent sales.

entries.scenario: The collections specialist at a packaged-goods wholesaler runs a daily dunning list of overdue invoices with customer email addresses to feed the reminder-email process.
entries.question: List all overdue invoices with the customer's email address and how many days late each one is.
entries.intent: Lists CustInvc headers with non-zero foreignamountunpaid and duedate before today, joined to customer for email, with computed days_overdue.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    c.entityid AS customer,
    c.email AS customer_email,
    t.tranid AS invoice_number,
    t.trandate AS invoice_date,
    t.duedate AS due_date,
    TRUNC(SYSDATE) - t.duedate AS days_overdue,
    t.foreignamountunpaid AS open_amount,
    BUILTIN.DF(t.currency) AS currency
FROM transaction t
JOIN customer c ON c.id = t.entity
WHERE t.type = 'CustInvc'
    AND t.foreignamountunpaid <> 0
    AND t.duedate < TRUNC(SYSDATE)
ORDER BY TRUNC(SYSDATE) - t.duedate DESC, t.foreignamountunpaid DESC
entries.caveats: Overdue = foreignamountunpaid <> 0 AND duedate < today; status letter codes are never filtered on. Invoices with NULL duedate are excluded by the duedate predicate — that is intentional for a dunning list but means no-terms invoices never appear. customer.email can be NULL; the email-send layer must handle blanks. open_amount is transaction currency.

entries.scenario: Before the weekly cash-flow meeting, the treasury analyst at a hardware distributor pulls the biggest open invoices to target high-impact collection calls.
entries.question: What are our :n largest open invoices right now, ranked in base currency?
entries.intent: Ranks open CustInvc headers by foreignamountunpaid converted to base currency via exchangerate, returning the top :n.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    t.tranid AS invoice_number,
    c.entityid AS customer,
    t.trandate AS invoice_date,
    t.duedate AS due_date,
    t.foreignamountunpaid AS open_amount,
    BUILTIN.DF(t.currency) AS currency,
    t.foreignamountunpaid * t.exchangerate AS open_amount_base
FROM transaction t
JOIN customer c ON c.id = t.entity
WHERE t.type = 'CustInvc'
    AND t.foreignamountunpaid <> 0
ORDER BY t.foreignamountunpaid * t.exchangerate DESC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: Ranking is on foreignamountunpaid * exchangerate so multi-currency invoices sort comparably; ranking raw foreignamountunpaid would mis-order across currencies. Uses FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY (never LIMIT/OFFSET). exchangerate converts to the transaction's own subsidiary base — with multiple base currencies, cross-subsidiary ranking is approximate.

entries.scenario: The AR team at a specialty-foods wholesaler produces month-end customer statements showing invoices, credit memos, and payments in chronological order per account.
entries.question: Build a statement-style activity list per customer showing invoices, credits, and payments between :start and :end with original and open amounts.
entries.intent: Chronological header-level listing of CustInvc, CustCred, and CustPymt per customer in a date range, showing signed original amount, remaining open amount, and display status.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    c.entityid AS customer,
    t.trandate AS tran_date,
    BUILTIN.DF(t.type) AS tran_type,
    t.tranid AS document_number,
    t.foreigntotal AS signed_amount,
    t.foreignamountunpaid AS open_amount,
    BUILTIN.DF(t.status) AS status,
    BUILTIN.DF(t.currency) AS currency
FROM transaction t
JOIN customer c ON c.id = t.entity
WHERE t.type IN ('CustInvc', 'CustCred', 'CustPymt')
    AND t.trandate BETWEEN TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD') AND TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
ORDER BY c.entityid, t.trandate, t.tranid
entries.caveats: Signs are native: CustInvc foreigntotal is positive; CustCred and CustPymt are credit-signed (negative), so summing signed_amount per customer yields the net AR movement for the period — do not ABS these rows on a statement. open_amount uses foreignamountunpaid; for payments a negative value means unapplied credit. Filter to a single customer at the application layer (only :start/:end/:n/:threshold/:period/:acctnumber placeholders are supported). Status is display-only via BUILTIN.DF.

entries.scenario: The finance manager of a distribution business reconciles cash receipts weekly and needs total customer payments received per customer for the period.
entries.question: How much did we receive in customer payments between :start and :end, by customer?
entries.intent: Aggregates CustPymt headers in a date range per customer: payment count, total received in base currency (negated because payments are credit-signed), and first/last payment dates.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    c.entityid AS customer,
    COUNT(t.id) AS payment_count,
    SUM(-t.foreigntotal * t.exchangerate) AS amount_received_base,
    MIN(t.trandate) AS first_payment_date,
    MAX(t.trandate) AS last_payment_date
FROM transaction t
JOIN customer c ON c.id = t.entity
WHERE t.type = 'CustPymt'
    AND t.trandate BETWEEN TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD') AND TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY c.entityid
ORDER BY SUM(-t.foreigntotal * t.exchangerate) DESC
entries.caveats: CustPymt foreigntotal is credit-signed (negative), so it is negated to report positive cash received — never SUM the raw value expecting a positive number. exchangerate converts each payment to its subsidiary base; consolidation across subsidiaries assumes a common base currency. This is header-grain cash received, not GL bank postings — use transactionaccountingline against Bank accounts for GL truth.

entries.scenario: During month-end close, the AR accountant at a consumer-electronics wholesaler hunts for customer payments that were never fully applied to invoices, since unapplied cash distorts customer balances.
entries.question: Which customer payments have unapplied amounts still sitting on account?
entries.intent: Lists CustPymt headers where foreignamountunpaid is non-zero, showing the original payment amount and the remaining unapplied portion (both negated to display positive).
entries.sql:
SELECT
    c.entityid AS customer,
    t.tranid AS payment_number,
    t.trandate AS payment_date,
    -t.foreigntotal AS payment_amount,
    -t.foreignamountunpaid AS unapplied_amount,
    BUILTIN.DF(t.status) AS status,
    BUILTIN.DF(t.currency) AS currency
FROM transaction t
JOIN customer c ON c.id = t.entity
WHERE t.type = 'CustPymt'
    AND t.foreignamountunpaid <> 0
ORDER BY t.trandate
entries.caveats: For credit-signed CustPymt rows, a non-zero foreignamountunpaid means unapplied credit and is NEGATIVE — both foreigntotal and foreignamountunpaid are negated for positive display. Filtering foreignamountunpaid <> 0 finds partially and fully unapplied payments without touching the raw status letter codes ('Deposited'/'Not Deposited' style states are display-only via BUILTIN.DF). Amounts are transaction currency.

entries.scenario: The credit manager at a multi-subsidiary distributor ranks the largest net debtor balances straight from the GL to set credit-limit reviews, and needs the numbers to tie to the AR control account.
entries.question: Who are our top :n debtors by net AR control-account balance above :threshold, split by subsidiary?
entries.intent: GL-grain ranking: sums signed transactionaccountingline.amount on posting rows against AcctRec-type accounts, grouped by customer and line subsidiary, keeping balances >= :threshold and returning the top :n.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    c.entityid AS customer,
    BUILTIN.DF(tl.subsidiary) AS subsidiary,
    SUM(tal.amount) AS ar_balance_base
FROM transactionaccountingline tal
JOIN transaction t ON t.id = tal.transaction
JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = tal.transaction
    AND tl.id = tal.transactionline
JOIN account a ON a.id = tal.account
JOIN customer c ON c.id = t.entity
WHERE tal.posting = 'T'
    AND a.accttype = 'AcctRec'
GROUP BY c.entityid, BUILTIN.DF(tl.subsidiary)
HAVING SUM(tal.amount) >= :threshold
ORDER BY SUM(tal.amount) DESC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: GL truth: posting='T' with signed tal.amount (AR debits positive, credits negative), so the plain SUM is the net receivable balance per customer — no per-type sign flipping needed at this grain. Subsidiary comes from transactionline (transaction.subsidiary is not queryable), and grouping by it keeps each base currency separate rather than mixing them. The inner join through t.entity to customer drops journal entries whose header entity is empty even if their lines hit AcctRec — such journal AR adjustments will not appear against a customer here. GROUP BY/HAVING/ORDER BY repeat full expressions, never aliases.

entries.scenario: The AP clerk at a wholesale distributor needs a live list of every unpaid or partially paid vendor bill to plan this week's check run and answer vendor calls.
entries.question: Show me all open vendor bills with vendor, due date, status, and the amount still owed.
entries.intent: Lists every vendor bill with a non-zero unpaid balance, sign-corrected to positive amounts, sorted by due date.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    t.tranid AS bill_number,
    v.entityid AS vendor_id,
    v.companyname AS vendor_name,
    t.trandate AS bill_date,
    t.duedate AS due_date,
    BUILTIN.DF(t.status) AS bill_status,
    -t.foreigntotal AS bill_total,
    -t.foreignamountunpaid AS amount_due
FROM transaction t
JOIN vendor v ON v.id = t.entity
WHERE t.type = 'VendBill'
    AND NVL(t.foreignamountunpaid, 0) <> 0
ORDER BY t.duedate, v.companyname
entries.caveats: Open filter uses foreignamountunpaid <> 0, never status='open' (status is a raw per-type letter code, displayed only via BUILTIN.DF). Vendor bill amounts are credit-signed (negative) so both foreigntotal and foreignamountunpaid are negated for positive display. Amounts are in transaction currency; no consolidation is attempted.

entries.scenario: The controller of a multi-brand retail products company reviews AP aging every Monday to decide which vendors to pay first and where to negotiate extended terms.
entries.question: Give me an AP aging report by vendor with buckets: current, 1-30, 31-60, 61-90, and over 90 days past due.
entries.intent: Nets open vendor bills and unapplied vendor credits per vendor into standard aging buckets keyed off duedate versus today.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    v.entityid AS vendor_id,
    v.companyname AS vendor_name,
    SUM(CASE WHEN TRUNC(SYSDATE) - NVL(t.duedate, t.trandate) <= 0
        THEN -t.foreignamountunpaid ELSE 0 END) AS current_not_due,
    SUM(CASE WHEN TRUNC(SYSDATE) - NVL(t.duedate, t.trandate) BETWEEN 1 AND 30
        THEN -t.foreignamountunpaid ELSE 0 END) AS days_1_30,
    SUM(CASE WHEN TRUNC(SYSDATE) - NVL(t.duedate, t.trandate) BETWEEN 31 AND 60
        THEN -t.foreignamountunpaid ELSE 0 END) AS days_31_60,
    SUM(CASE WHEN TRUNC(SYSDATE) - NVL(t.duedate, t.trandate) BETWEEN 61 AND 90
        THEN -t.foreignamountunpaid ELSE 0 END) AS days_61_90,
    SUM(CASE WHEN TRUNC(SYSDATE) - NVL(t.duedate, t.trandate) > 90
        THEN -t.foreignamountunpaid ELSE 0 END) AS days_over_90,
    SUM(-t.foreignamountunpaid) AS total_open
FROM transaction t
JOIN vendor v ON v.id = t.entity
WHERE t.type IN ('VendBill', 'VendCred')
    AND NVL(t.foreignamountunpaid, 0) <> 0
GROUP BY v.entityid, v.companyname
ORDER BY SUM(-t.foreignamountunpaid) DESC
entries.caveats: Openness is foreignamountunpaid <> 0, not a status string. Bills carry a negative unpaid balance and unapplied vendor credits a positive one, so negating foreignamountunpaid yields positive AP for bills and negative offsets for credits, netting to true vendor exposure. NVL(duedate, trandate) covers bills entered without terms. Amounts are transaction-currency; a mixed-currency vendor mixes currencies in one row.

entries.scenario: A procurement director at a consumer goods wholesaler wants a GL-verified top-vendor spend ranking for the annual supplier negotiation cycle, netting out any credit memos.
entries.question: Rank our top vendors by posted expense and COGS spend between two dates, net of vendor credits.
entries.intent: Ranks vendors by net posted GL spend (debit - credit on Expense/COGS/OthExpense accounts) from vendor bills and credits in a date range, top :n.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    v.entityid AS vendor_id,
    v.companyname AS vendor_name,
    SUM(tal.amount) AS net_gl_spend,
    COUNT(DISTINCT t.id) AS transaction_count
FROM transaction t
JOIN transactionaccountingline tal ON tal.transaction = t.id
JOIN account a ON a.id = tal.account
JOIN vendor v ON v.id = t.entity
WHERE t.type IN ('VendBill', 'VendCred')
    AND tal.posting = 'T'
    AND a.accttype IN ('Expense', 'COGS', 'OthExpense')
    AND t.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND t.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY v.entityid, v.companyname
ORDER BY SUM(tal.amount) DESC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: GL truth grain: transactionaccountingline with posting='T'. tal.amount is signed debit-positive, so expense debits on bills come through positive and vendor-credit reversals come through negative — SUM(tal.amount) is already net spend with no manual sign flip. accttype restricted to expense-side types so inventory receipts to asset accounts are excluded. FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY (never LIMIT/OFFSET). Cross-subsidiary amounts are in each transaction's base ledger currency.

entries.scenario: The treasury analyst at a distribution company reconciles weekly payment runs against the bank and needs a per-day, per-currency summary of vendor payments issued.
entries.question: Summarize vendor payment runs by day: how many payments went out, how many vendors were paid, and the total per currency?
entries.intent: Aggregates VendPymt transactions by payment date and currency with payment count, distinct vendor count, and total paid.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    t.trandate AS payment_date,
    c.name AS currency_name,
    COUNT(DISTINCT t.id) AS payment_count,
    COUNT(DISTINCT t.entity) AS vendors_paid,
    SUM(ABS(t.foreigntotal)) AS total_paid
FROM transaction t
JOIN currency c ON c.id = t.currency
WHERE t.type = 'VendPymt'
    AND t.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND t.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY t.trandate, c.name
ORDER BY t.trandate DESC, c.name
entries.caveats: ABS(foreigntotal) makes the query robust to AP sign conventions on payment headers (bills are credit-signed; payments sit on the opposite side). Grouping by currency prevents silently summing mixed transaction currencies in one figure. Voided payments still on file will appear; layer BUILTIN.DF(t.status) in a detail view if void exclusion is required.

entries.scenario: An AP manager at a wholesale products company builds a short-term cash forecast every Friday and needs every bill coming due within a chosen horizon.
entries.question: Which vendor bills are due in the next N days, and how much cash do we need for each?
entries.intent: Lists open vendor bills with a due date between today and today + :threshold days, with positive amounts due and a days-until-due countdown.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    t.duedate AS due_date,
    TRUNC(t.duedate) - TRUNC(SYSDATE) AS days_until_due,
    t.tranid AS bill_number,
    v.companyname AS vendor_name,
    BUILTIN.DF(t.status) AS bill_status,
    -t.foreignamountunpaid AS amount_due
FROM transaction t
JOIN vendor v ON v.id = t.entity
WHERE t.type = 'VendBill'
    AND NVL(t.foreignamountunpaid, 0) <> 0
    AND t.duedate >= TRUNC(SYSDATE)
    AND t.duedate <= TRUNC(SYSDATE) + :threshold
ORDER BY t.duedate, v.companyname
entries.caveats: :threshold is repurposed as the days-ahead horizon (numeric), the only sanctioned numeric placeholder besides :n. Open filter is foreignamountunpaid <> 0 (never a status string); unpaid amount is negated because bill balances are credit-signed. Bills already past due are deliberately excluded (duedate >= today) — combine with the aging query for the overdue picture. Date arithmetic on TRUNC'd dates keeps whole-day counts.

entries.scenario: A category manager at a retail products company tracks month-over-month billing from each supplier to spot cost creep and seasonal buying patterns before quarterly business reviews.
entries.question: Show net spend by vendor by month over a date range, including the offset from any vendor credits.
entries.intent: Monthly net billed amount per vendor: vendor bill totals minus vendor credit totals, grouped on TO_CHAR(trandate,'YYYY-MM').
entries.sql:
SELECT
    v.companyname AS vendor_name,
    TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM') AS spend_month,
    SUM(CASE WHEN t.type = 'VendBill' THEN ABS(t.foreigntotal)
        ELSE -ABS(t.foreigntotal) END) AS net_spend,
    COUNT(DISTINCT t.id) AS document_count
FROM transaction t
JOIN vendor v ON v.id = t.entity
WHERE t.type IN ('VendBill', 'VendCred')
    AND t.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND t.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY v.companyname, TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM')
ORDER BY v.companyname, TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM')
entries.caveats: CASE + ABS makes the sign handling convention-proof: bills always add positively and credits always subtract, regardless of the credit-signed storage of vendor bill headers. Month bucket TO_CHAR(trandate,'YYYY-MM') is repeated verbatim in GROUP BY and ORDER BY (no aliases in GROUP BY). Amounts are transaction currency; multiply by t.exchangerate if a base-currency view is needed.

entries.scenario: The CFO of a wholesale distributor is assessing supplier risk and wants to know how concentrated purchasing is: what share of total billed spend the top vendors represent.
entries.question: What percentage of our total vendor bill spend does each of our top N vendors represent for a given period?
entries.intent: Ranks vendors by billed spend in a date range and computes each vendor's percentage of total spend via a derived total-spend cross join, top :n.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    vs.vendor_name,
    vs.vendor_spend,
    tot.total_spend,
    ROUND(100 * vs.vendor_spend / tot.total_spend, 2) AS pct_of_total_spend
FROM (
    SELECT
        v.companyname AS vendor_name,
        SUM(-t.foreigntotal) AS vendor_spend
    FROM transaction t
    JOIN vendor v ON v.id = t.entity
    WHERE t.type = 'VendBill'
        AND t.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        AND t.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    GROUP BY v.companyname
) vs,
(
    SELECT SUM(-t2.foreigntotal) AS total_spend
    FROM transaction t2
    WHERE t2.type = 'VendBill'
        AND t2.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        AND t2.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
) tot
ORDER BY vs.vendor_spend DESC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: No window functions assumed: the denominator comes from a second derived table cross-joined (comma join) to the per-vendor rollup, with identical :start/:end filters so numerator and denominator cover the same population. Bill headers are credit-signed, hence SUM(-foreigntotal) for positive spend. Vendor credits are excluded from both sides to keep the ratio consistent. Percentages mix transaction currencies if the account is multi-currency — state base-currency needs before trusting the ratio across subsidiaries.

entries.scenario: The treasury lead at a multi-entity products company reconciles AP cash outflow to the general ledger each close, needing posted vendor-payment cash by bank account and accounting period.
entries.question: How much vendor payment cash left each bank account per accounting period?
entries.intent: Posted GL cash outflow from VendPymt transactions: credit side of Bank accounts grouped by accountingperiod (period name) and bank account.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    p.periodname AS posting_period,
    a.acctnumber AS account_number,
    a.fullname AS bank_account,
    SUM(-tal.amount) AS cash_paid_out,
    COUNT(DISTINCT t.id) AS payment_count
FROM transaction t
JOIN transactionaccountingline tal ON tal.transaction = t.id
JOIN account a ON a.id = tal.account
JOIN accountingperiod p ON p.id = t.postingperiod
WHERE t.type = 'VendPymt'
    AND tal.posting = 'T'
    AND a.accttype = 'Bank'
    AND p.isadjust = 'F'
    AND p.startdate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND p.startdate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY p.periodname, p.startdate, a.acctnumber, a.fullname
ORDER BY p.startdate, a.acctnumber
entries.caveats: GL grain with posting='T' only — this ties to the ledger, not to header totals. Vendor payments credit the bank account, so tal.amount is negative on those lines and SUM(-amount) yields positive cash out. Period join goes through transaction.postingperiod with isadjust='F' to skip adjustment rollup rows; p.startdate is added to GROUP BY so ORDER BY can sort chronologically without aliases. Amounts are in each transaction's base ledger currency.

entries.scenario: The FP&A team of a multi-subsidiary wholesale group allocates supplier spend to operating units and needs a line-level breakdown of vendor bill spend by subsidiary and department in base currency.
entries.question: Break down vendor bill spend by subsidiary, vendor, and department in base currency for a date range.
entries.intent: Line-grain vendor bill spend converted with transaction.exchangerate, grouped by transactionline subsidiary, vendor, and department.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    BUILTIN.DF(tl.subsidiary) AS subsidiary_name,
    v.companyname AS vendor_name,
    BUILTIN.DF(tl.department) AS department_name,
    SUM(tl.netamount * t.exchangerate) AS base_currency_spend,
    COUNT(DISTINCT t.id) AS bill_count
FROM transaction t
JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = t.id
JOIN vendor v ON v.id = t.entity
WHERE t.type = 'VendBill'
    AND tl.mainline = 'F'
    AND tl.taxline = 'F'
    AND t.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND t.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY BUILTIN.DF(tl.subsidiary), v.companyname, BUILTIN.DF(tl.department)
ORDER BY BUILTIN.DF(tl.subsidiary), SUM(tl.netamount * t.exchangerate) DESC
entries.caveats: Subsidiary and department live on transactionline, not the header (transaction.subsidiary is NOT queryable) — both displayed via BUILTIN.DF and repeated verbatim in GROUP BY/ORDER BY. mainline='F' AND taxline='F' prevents double counting header and tax rows. Purchase-side line netamount is debit-positive (unlike sales lines), so no negation is needed. exchangerate converts each line to its own subsidiary's base currency; cross-subsidiary totals therefore mix base currencies unless all subsidiaries share one — that assumption is why results stay grouped by subsidiary.

entries.scenario: A staff accountant at a wholesale products distributor needs to see how a single GL account (e.g. Freight Expense) has trended month by month before proposing a reclass during close.
entries.question: Show the posted debit, credit, and net activity for account number :acctnumber by accounting period between :start and :end.
entries.intent: Per-period posted debit/credit/net GL activity for one account, keyed by posting period (not transaction date).
entries.sql:
SELECT
    ap.periodname AS period_name,
    ap.startdate AS period_start,
    SUM(CASE WHEN tal.amount > 0 THEN tal.amount ELSE 0 END) AS total_debits,
    SUM(CASE WHEN tal.amount < 0 THEN -tal.amount ELSE 0 END) AS total_credits,
    SUM(tal.amount) AS net_activity
FROM transactionaccountingline tal
JOIN transaction t ON t.id = tal.transaction
JOIN accountingperiod ap ON ap.id = t.postingperiod
JOIN account a ON a.id = tal.account
WHERE tal.posting = 'T'
    AND a.acctnumber = :acctnumber
    AND ap.isadjust = 'F'
    AND ap.isquarter = 'F'
    AND ap.isyear = 'F'
    AND ap.startdate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND ap.startdate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY ap.periodname, ap.startdate
ORDER BY ap.startdate
entries.caveats: GL truth comes from transactionaccountingline with posting='T' (never transactionline netamount for ledger balances). tal.amount is signed: positive = debit, negative = credit, so debit/credit columns are split with CASE and credits are negated for display. Period join goes through transaction.postingperiod, and accountingperiod is filtered isadjust='F' AND isquarter='F' AND isyear='F' to exclude rollup rows that would double-count. All expressions are repeated verbatim in GROUP BY (no aliases).

entries.scenario: The controller of a retail products company wants a quick trial-balance-style sanity check for one accounting period during month-end close, confirming that total posted debits equal total posted credits across account types.
entries.question: Give me trial-balance style totals — total debits, total credits, and net — grouped by account type for the period :period.
entries.intent: Posted debit/credit/net totals per account.accttype for a single named accounting period; grand debits should equal grand credits.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    a.accttype AS account_type,
    COUNT(tal.transaction) AS posted_line_count,
    SUM(CASE WHEN tal.amount > 0 THEN tal.amount ELSE 0 END) AS total_debits,
    SUM(CASE WHEN tal.amount < 0 THEN -tal.amount ELSE 0 END) AS total_credits,
    SUM(tal.amount) AS net_debit_minus_credit
FROM transactionaccountingline tal
JOIN transaction t ON t.id = tal.transaction
JOIN accountingperiod ap ON ap.id = t.postingperiod
JOIN account a ON a.id = tal.account
WHERE tal.posting = 'T'
    AND ap.periodname = :period
    AND ap.isadjust = 'F'
GROUP BY a.accttype
ORDER BY a.accttype
entries.caveats: Uses transactionaccountingline with posting='T' as the GL source of truth. tal.amount sign convention (debit +, credit -) drives the CASE split; net_debit_minus_credit summed across all rows should be zero if the ledger balances. accountingperiod filtered isadjust='F' so adjustment rollups don't inflate totals; :period matches a single month periodname so isquarter/isyear filters are unnecessary but the name must be a base period like 'Jan 2026'. Amounts are ledger (base-currency) postings per each transaction's subsidiary ledger — across subsidiaries this mixes base currencies.

entries.scenario: The FP&A analyst at a consumer goods wholesaler builds a monthly P&L trend to spot margin drift: revenue, COGS, operating expense, and net income by month for the trailing year.
entries.question: Build a P&L by month between :start and :end: revenue, COGS, operating expenses, and net income per accounting period.
entries.intent: Monthly income statement from posted GL lines, with income shown positive via credit-minus-debit and expenses positive via debit-minus-credit.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    ap.periodname AS period_name,
    ap.startdate AS period_start,
    SUM(CASE WHEN a.accttype IN ('Income', 'OthIncome') THEN -tal.amount ELSE 0 END) AS revenue,
    SUM(CASE WHEN a.accttype = 'COGS' THEN tal.amount ELSE 0 END) AS cogs,
    SUM(CASE WHEN a.accttype IN ('Income', 'OthIncome') THEN -tal.amount WHEN a.accttype = 'COGS' THEN -tal.amount ELSE 0 END) AS gross_profit,
    SUM(CASE WHEN a.accttype IN ('Expense', 'OthExpense') THEN tal.amount ELSE 0 END) AS operating_expense,
    SUM(-tal.amount) AS net_income
FROM transactionaccountingline tal
JOIN transaction t ON t.id = tal.transaction
JOIN accountingperiod ap ON ap.id = t.postingperiod
JOIN account a ON a.id = tal.account
WHERE tal.posting = 'T'
    AND a.accttype IN ('Income', 'OthIncome', 'COGS', 'Expense', 'OthExpense')
    AND ap.isadjust = 'F'
    AND ap.isquarter = 'F'
    AND ap.isyear = 'F'
    AND ap.startdate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND ap.startdate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY ap.periodname, ap.startdate
ORDER BY ap.startdate
entries.caveats: Sign discipline is the whole trick: tal.amount is debit-positive/credit-negative, so income = credit - debit = -amount (negated to show revenue positive) and expense = debit - credit = +amount. Because the WHERE clause restricts to P&L accttypes only, net_income collapses to SUM(-tal.amount) for all rows. Uses posting periods (transaction.postingperiod), not trandate, so late-posted entries land in the correct month; rollup periods excluded via isadjust/isquarter/isyear='F'. Consolidates across subsidiaries in each ledger's base currency — mixed-currency accounts should add a subsidiary grouping (see FIN-GL-009).

entries.scenario: An operations accountant at a distribution company tracks document throughput — how many invoices, bills, payments, and journals were entered each month — to plan close staffing and spot entry backlogs.
entries.question: How many transactions of each type were created per month between :start and :end?
entries.intent: Header-grain count of transactions by month (trandate) and transaction type, with human-readable type names.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM') AS tran_month,
    t.type AS type_code,
    BUILTIN.DF(t.type) AS type_name,
    COUNT(t.id) AS tran_count
FROM transaction t
WHERE t.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND t.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM'), t.type, BUILTIN.DF(t.type)
ORDER BY TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM'), COUNT(t.id) DESC
entries.caveats: Pure header grain — counting transaction rows directly avoids the line-fanout that a transactionline join would cause. Raw t.type codes (CustInvc, VendBill, ...) are kept for filtering/programmatic use while BUILTIN.DF(t.type) gives the display name. Bucketing is by trandate (entry activity), not posting period — non-posting documents like Sales Orders are intentionally included; add a posting filter if only GL-posting docs matter. Expressions are repeated in GROUP BY and ORDER BY (no aliases), per SuiteQL rules.

entries.scenario: During an audit request, a senior accountant at a products wholesaler must produce the full posted GL detail for one account — every debit and credit with source document and counterparty — over a date range.
entries.question: List every posted GL entry hitting account :acctnumber between :start and :end, with date, document, entity, period, and debit/credit columns.
entries.intent: Line-level posted GL register (detail listing) for a single account over a trandate range, formatted with separate debit and credit columns.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    t.trandate AS tran_date,
    BUILTIN.DF(t.type) AS tran_type,
    t.tranid AS document_number,
    BUILTIN.DF(t.entity) AS entity_name,
    ap.periodname AS posting_period,
    CASE WHEN tal.amount > 0 THEN tal.amount ELSE 0 END AS debit,
    CASE WHEN tal.amount < 0 THEN -tal.amount ELSE 0 END AS credit,
    tal.amount AS signed_amount
FROM transactionaccountingline tal
JOIN transaction t ON t.id = tal.transaction
JOIN account a ON a.id = tal.account
JOIN accountingperiod ap ON ap.id = t.postingperiod
WHERE tal.posting = 'T'
    AND a.acctnumber = :acctnumber
    AND t.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND t.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
ORDER BY t.trandate, t.id
entries.caveats: transaction.entity is polymorphic (customer, vendor, employee...), so BUILTIN.DF(t.entity) is used for the display name instead of a type-specific join that would drop rows for other entity types. Debit/credit presentation derives from the signed tal.amount (debit +, credit -); posting='T' excludes non-posting and voided memory lines. Detail is filtered by trandate for an activity window — switch the filter to ap.periodname if the auditor wants posting-period cutoff instead.

entries.scenario: A finance manager at a hardgoods retailer investigates why this month's expenses jumped versus a prior month: she needs each account's posted activity in both months side by side with the movement, ranked by biggest swing.
entries.question: Compare posted net activity per account between the month containing :start and the month containing :end, and rank accounts by the size of the movement.
entries.intent: Per-account net GL activity in two specific monthly periods with the delta, ordered by absolute movement descending, top :n rows.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    a.acctnumber AS account_number,
    a.fullname AS account_name,
    a.accttype AS account_type,
    SUM(CASE WHEN ap.startdate = TRUNC(TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD'), 'MM') THEN tal.amount ELSE 0 END) AS activity_month_1,
    SUM(CASE WHEN ap.startdate = TRUNC(TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD'), 'MM') THEN tal.amount ELSE 0 END) AS activity_month_2,
    SUM(CASE WHEN ap.startdate = TRUNC(TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD'), 'MM') THEN tal.amount ELSE 0 END)
        - SUM(CASE WHEN ap.startdate = TRUNC(TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD'), 'MM') THEN tal.amount ELSE 0 END) AS movement
FROM transactionaccountingline tal
JOIN transaction t ON t.id = tal.transaction
JOIN accountingperiod ap ON ap.id = t.postingperiod
JOIN account a ON a.id = tal.account
WHERE tal.posting = 'T'
    AND ap.isadjust = 'F'
    AND ap.isquarter = 'F'
    AND ap.isyear = 'F'
    AND (ap.startdate = TRUNC(TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD'), 'MM')
        OR ap.startdate = TRUNC(TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD'), 'MM'))
GROUP BY a.acctnumber, a.fullname, a.accttype
ORDER BY ABS(SUM(CASE WHEN ap.startdate = TRUNC(TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD'), 'MM') THEN tal.amount ELSE 0 END)
    - SUM(CASE WHEN ap.startdate = TRUNC(TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD'), 'MM') THEN tal.amount ELSE 0 END)) DESC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: Two months are pivoted into columns with conditional SUMs so a single GL pass produces the comparison; the two target periods are identified by matching accountingperiod.startdate to TRUNC(date,'MM'), which assumes standard calendar-month periods starting on the 1st. Rollup periods excluded (isadjust/isquarter/isyear='F') so quarter/year rows never match. Amounts are signed net activity (debit - credit) — for P&L accounts a positive movement means more expense or less income depending on accttype. Full CASE expressions are repeated in ORDER BY because aliases cannot be referenced there with aggregates in SuiteQL.

entries.scenario: Before signing off on the close, a controller at a wholesale distributor scans the largest individual GL postings in the period to catch fat-finger entries, mis-keyed journals, or unusual one-time hits.
entries.question: What are the :n largest single posted GL line impacts (by absolute amount) in period :period, with account, document, and status?
entries.intent: Top-N individual transactionaccountingline rows in one posting period ranked by ABS(amount), with document and account context.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    t.trandate AS tran_date,
    BUILTIN.DF(t.type) AS tran_type,
    t.tranid AS document_number,
    a.acctnumber AS account_number,
    a.fullname AS account_name,
    a.accttype AS account_type,
    tal.amount AS signed_amount,
    ABS(tal.amount) AS impact_magnitude,
    BUILTIN.DF(t.status) AS status
FROM transactionaccountingline tal
JOIN transaction t ON t.id = tal.transaction
JOIN accountingperiod ap ON ap.id = t.postingperiod
JOIN account a ON a.id = tal.account
WHERE tal.posting = 'T'
    AND ap.periodname = :period
ORDER BY ABS(tal.amount) DESC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: Ranks raw posted GL lines, so a large journal appears once per large leg (debit and credit legs both surface — useful for review). Status is displayed with BUILTIN.DF(t.status) because raw status codes are per-type letters and must never be compared to words like 'open'. Uses FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY (never LIMIT/OFFSET). Signed amount is kept alongside the magnitude so reviewers can tell debits from credits.

entries.scenario: A department-budget owner review at a retail products company: FP&A needs posted operating expense by department by month to feed the budget-vs-actual pack, including spend never tagged to a department.
entries.question: Show posted operating expense by department for each month between :start and :end, including an 'Unassigned' bucket.
entries.intent: Monthly posted expense (Expense + OthExpense accounts) grouped by transactionline department, with untagged lines bucketed as Unassigned.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    ap.periodname AS period_name,
    ap.startdate AS period_start,
    NVL(BUILTIN.DF(tl.department), 'Unassigned') AS department_name,
    SUM(tal.amount) AS expense_amount
FROM transactionaccountingline tal
JOIN transaction t ON t.id = tal.transaction
JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = tal.transaction AND tl.id = tal.transactionline
JOIN account a ON a.id = tal.account
JOIN accountingperiod ap ON ap.id = t.postingperiod
WHERE tal.posting = 'T'
    AND a.accttype IN ('Expense', 'OthExpense')
    AND ap.isadjust = 'F'
    AND ap.isquarter = 'F'
    AND ap.isyear = 'F'
    AND ap.startdate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND ap.startdate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY ap.periodname, ap.startdate, NVL(BUILTIN.DF(tl.department), 'Unassigned')
ORDER BY ap.startdate, SUM(tal.amount) DESC
entries.caveats: Department lives on transactionline, not the transaction header, so the GL line is joined back to its source line on BOTH keys (tal.transaction = tl.transaction AND tal.transactionline = tl.id) — joining on transaction alone would fan out and multiply amounts. Expense sign: debit - credit = +tal.amount, so no negation needed for Expense/OthExpense accttypes. NVL wraps BUILTIN.DF so untagged spend is visible rather than silently grouped under NULL; the full NVL expression is repeated in GROUP BY. Amounts are ledger base-currency postings; restrict tl.subsidiary if a single-entity view is required.

entries.scenario: The CFO of a multi-entity wholesale group wants a per-subsidiary P&L snapshot for a date range to see which entities are profitable before consolidation adjustments.
entries.question: Break down revenue, COGS, operating expense, and net income by subsidiary for posting periods between :start and :end.
entries.intent: P&L rollup (revenue/COGS/opex/net income) grouped by transactionline subsidiary over a posting-period date range, in each subsidiary's base currency.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    BUILTIN.DF(tl.subsidiary) AS subsidiary_name,
    SUM(CASE WHEN a.accttype IN ('Income', 'OthIncome') THEN -tal.amount ELSE 0 END) AS revenue,
    SUM(CASE WHEN a.accttype = 'COGS' THEN tal.amount ELSE 0 END) AS cogs,
    SUM(CASE WHEN a.accttype IN ('Expense', 'OthExpense') THEN tal.amount ELSE 0 END) AS operating_expense,
    SUM(-tal.amount) AS net_income
FROM transactionaccountingline tal
JOIN transaction t ON t.id = tal.transaction
JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = tal.transaction AND tl.id = tal.transactionline
JOIN account a ON a.id = tal.account
JOIN accountingperiod ap ON ap.id = t.postingperiod
WHERE tal.posting = 'T'
    AND a.accttype IN ('Income', 'OthIncome', 'COGS', 'Expense', 'OthExpense')
    AND ap.isadjust = 'F'
    AND ap.isquarter = 'F'
    AND ap.isyear = 'F'
    AND ap.startdate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND ap.startdate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY BUILTIN.DF(tl.subsidiary)
ORDER BY SUM(-tal.amount) DESC
entries.caveats: transaction.subsidiary is NOT queryable in SuiteQL (NOT_EXPOSED); subsidiary must come from transactionline via BUILTIN.DF(tl.subsidiary), with the GL-to-line join on both keys to avoid fanout. Each subsidiary's figures are in that subsidiary's own base currency — rows are directly comparable only if all subsidiaries share a base currency; do NOT sum rows across subsidiaries for a consolidated total without translation. Sign convention: income shown positive as -amount (credit - debit), expenses positive as +amount; net_income = SUM(-tal.amount) since the WHERE restricts to P&L accttypes. Rollup periods excluded via isadjust/isquarter/isyear='F'.

entries.scenario: The controller at a wholesale distribution company prepares the monthly sales tax remittance and needs the tax charged on customer billing documents totaled by accounting period.
entries.question: How much sales tax did we charge customers each month, based on the tax lines of our invoices and credit memos?
entries.intent: Sums transactionline tax lines (taxline='T') on posted CustInvc/CustCred by posting period, sign-corrected to positive tax collected.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    ap.periodname AS period_name,
    SUM(-tl.netamount) AS tax_collected,
    COUNT(DISTINCT t.id) AS taxed_transactions
FROM transaction t
JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = t.id
JOIN accountingperiod ap ON ap.id = t.postingperiod
WHERE t.posting = 'T'
    AND t.type IN ('CustInvc', 'CustCred')
    AND tl.taxline = 'T'
    AND ap.isadjust = 'F'
    AND ap.startdate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND ap.startdate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY ap.periodname, ap.startdate
ORDER BY ap.startdate
entries.caveats: Deliberately selects ONLY tax lines with tl.taxline='T' (the inverse of the usual taxline='F' filter used when summing item lines). Sales-side netamount is credit-signed negative, so SUM(-tl.netamount) yields positive tax collected; credit memo tax lines carry the opposite sign and correctly reduce the net. Period joined via transaction.postingperiod with isadjust='F' to skip adjustment periods. Amounts are in transaction currency (no exchangerate applied) — see FIN-TAXPER-007 for base-currency version.

entries.scenario: A finance manager at a retail products company drafts the quarterly VAT/GST return and wants a per-period proxy of output tax charged on sales versus input tax paid on vendor bills to estimate the net remittance.
entries.question: Compare the tax we collected on sales against the tax we paid on purchases per period, and show the net tax position.
entries.intent: Per posting period: CASE-split sum of taxline='T' lines into tax collected (CustInvc/CustCred, negated) vs tax paid (VendBill/VendCred, natural sign) plus the net difference.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    ap.periodname AS period_name,
    SUM(CASE WHEN t.type IN ('CustInvc', 'CustCred') THEN -tl.netamount ELSE 0 END) AS tax_collected_sales,
    SUM(CASE WHEN t.type IN ('VendBill', 'VendCred') THEN tl.netamount ELSE 0 END) AS tax_paid_purchases,
    SUM(CASE WHEN t.type IN ('CustInvc', 'CustCred') THEN -tl.netamount ELSE 0 END)
        - SUM(CASE WHEN t.type IN ('VendBill', 'VendCred') THEN tl.netamount ELSE 0 END) AS net_tax_position
FROM transaction t
JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = t.id
JOIN accountingperiod ap ON ap.id = t.postingperiod
WHERE t.posting = 'T'
    AND t.type IN ('CustInvc', 'CustCred', 'VendBill', 'VendCred')
    AND tl.taxline = 'T'
    AND ap.isadjust = 'F'
    AND ap.startdate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND ap.startdate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY ap.periodname, ap.startdate
ORDER BY ap.startdate
entries.caveats: Signs differ by side: sales tax lines are credit-signed negative so they are negated; purchase-side tax lines on VendBill are debit-signed positive and taken as-is, with VendCred tax lines carrying the opposite sign and netting naturally. This is a PROXY for a tax return — it ignores tax on journals, expense reports, and cash transactions, and mixes transaction currencies. Adjustment periods excluded via isadjust='F'.

entries.scenario: The accounting manager at a consumer goods wholesaler runs a month-end close checklist and needs to see which accounting periods are still open versus closed, alongside how much GL activity has posted into each.
entries.question: Show each month period in the range with its open/closed status and the total posted debits, credits, and transaction count.
entries.intent: Lists base month periods (rollups excluded) with a derived Open/Closed label and LEFT-JOINed posted GL debit/credit totals from transactionaccountingline.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    ap.periodname AS period_name,
    ap.startdate AS period_start,
    CASE WHEN ap.closed = 'T' THEN 'Closed' ELSE 'Open' END AS period_status,
    COUNT(DISTINCT t.id) AS posted_transactions,
    NVL(SUM(CASE WHEN tal.amount > 0 THEN tal.amount ELSE 0 END), 0) AS total_debits,
    NVL(SUM(CASE WHEN tal.amount < 0 THEN -tal.amount ELSE 0 END), 0) AS total_credits
FROM accountingperiod ap
LEFT JOIN transaction t ON t.postingperiod = ap.id AND t.posting = 'T'
LEFT JOIN transactionaccountingline tal ON tal.transaction = t.id AND tal.posting = 'T'
WHERE ap.isadjust = 'F'
    AND ap.isquarter = 'F'
    AND ap.isyear = 'F'
    AND ap.startdate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND ap.startdate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY ap.periodname, ap.startdate, CASE WHEN ap.closed = 'T' THEN 'Closed' ELSE 'Open' END
ORDER BY ap.startdate
entries.caveats: Month periods isolated with isadjust='F' AND isquarter='F' AND isyear='F' so quarter/year rollup rows never appear. LEFT JOINs keep periods with zero postings visible (NVL guards the sums). Debits/credits split from the single signed tal.amount (debit +, credit -). The posting filters live in the JOIN ON clauses, not WHERE, so they do not silently turn the LEFT JOINs into inner joins. The CASE expression is repeated verbatim in GROUP BY (no alias references allowed).

entries.scenario: The CFO of a multi-channel products company reviews monthly revenue momentum and wants each period's GL revenue next to the prior period's, with the absolute and percentage change.
entries.question: What is our posted revenue by month, and how does each month compare to the previous month in dollars and percent?
entries.intent: Self-joins a per-period GL income aggregate to itself on ADD_MONTHS(startdate,-1) to produce period-over-period revenue deltas and percent change.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    cur.periodname AS period_name,
    cur.revenue AS revenue,
    NVL(prev.revenue, 0) AS prior_period_revenue,
    cur.revenue - NVL(prev.revenue, 0) AS revenue_delta,
    CASE WHEN NVL(prev.revenue, 0) = 0 THEN NULL
        ELSE ROUND((cur.revenue - prev.revenue) / ABS(prev.revenue) * 100, 2) END AS pct_change
FROM (
    SELECT ap.periodname AS periodname, ap.startdate AS startdate, SUM(-tal.amount) AS revenue
    FROM transactionaccountingline tal
    JOIN transaction t ON t.id = tal.transaction
    JOIN account a ON a.id = tal.account
    JOIN accountingperiod ap ON ap.id = t.postingperiod
    WHERE tal.posting = 'T'
        AND a.accttype = 'Income'
        AND ap.isadjust = 'F'
    GROUP BY ap.periodname, ap.startdate
) cur
LEFT JOIN (
    SELECT ap.periodname AS periodname, ap.startdate AS startdate, SUM(-tal.amount) AS revenue
    FROM transactionaccountingline tal
    JOIN transaction t ON t.id = tal.transaction
    JOIN account a ON a.id = tal.account
    JOIN accountingperiod ap ON ap.id = t.postingperiod
    WHERE tal.posting = 'T'
        AND a.accttype = 'Income'
        AND ap.isadjust = 'F'
    GROUP BY ap.periodname, ap.startdate
) prev ON prev.startdate = ADD_MONTHS(cur.startdate, -1)
WHERE cur.startdate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND cur.startdate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
ORDER BY cur.startdate
entries.caveats: GL truth: revenue = credit - debit, i.e. SUM(-tal.amount) on posting='T' Income accounts — not header foreigntotal. Prior period matched via ADD_MONTHS on period startdate instead of window functions (no LAG dependence). LEFT JOIN keeps the first in-range month with a zero prior; pct_change is NULL when the prior is zero to avoid divide-by-zero. tal.amount is in each transaction's subsidiary base currency — cross-subsidiary totals assume a common base; group by BUILTIN.DF(tl.subsidiary) if that assumption fails. Adjustment periods excluded (isadjust='F').

entries.scenario: An internal auditor at a distribution company reviews close discipline and wants to catch backdated or forward-posted documents where the transaction date falls in a different month than the accounting period it posted to.
entries.question: Which posted transactions have a transaction date in a different month than their posting period?
entries.intent: Lists posted transactions where TO_CHAR(trandate,'YYYY-MM') differs from the posting period's start month, with type, subsidiary, and document total.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    t.tranid AS document_number,
    BUILTIN.DF(t.type) AS transaction_type,
    t.trandate AS transaction_date,
    TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM') AS trandate_month,
    ap.periodname AS posting_period,
    BUILTIN.DF(tl.subsidiary) AS subsidiary,
    t.foreigntotal AS document_total
FROM transaction t
JOIN accountingperiod ap ON ap.id = t.postingperiod
JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = t.id AND tl.mainline = 'T'
WHERE t.posting = 'T'
    AND ap.isadjust = 'F'
    AND TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM') <> TO_CHAR(ap.startdate, 'YYYY-MM')
    AND t.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND t.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
ORDER BY t.trandate DESC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: Subsidiary comes from transactionline (mainline='T' gives exactly one header row per transaction) because transaction.subsidiary is not exposed to SuiteQL. Month comparison uses the period's startdate month, which assumes standard month-length base periods; adjustment periods are excluded via isadjust='F' so legitimate 'Adjust' postings are not flagged. document_total is raw foreigntotal — credit-signed (negative) for vendor bills. FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY caps the list (never LIMIT/OFFSET).

entries.scenario: The FP&A lead at a wholesale products company builds a quarterly P&L summary but has been burned by NetSuite's quarter rollup period rows double-counting; they want quarters computed strictly from the underlying month periods.
entries.question: Give me revenue, COGS, operating expense, and gross profit rolled up by fiscal quarter, computed from the month periods only.
entries.intent: Aggregates posted GL amounts from base month periods into quarter buckets derived from period startdate (YYYY-Q), never touching isquarter rollup rows.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    TO_CHAR(ap.startdate, 'YYYY') || '-Q' || TO_CHAR(ap.startdate, 'Q') AS fiscal_quarter,
    SUM(CASE WHEN a.accttype = 'Income' THEN -tal.amount ELSE 0 END) AS revenue,
    SUM(CASE WHEN a.accttype = 'COGS' THEN tal.amount ELSE 0 END) AS cogs,
    SUM(CASE WHEN a.accttype IN ('Expense', 'OthExpense') THEN tal.amount ELSE 0 END) AS operating_expense,
    SUM(CASE WHEN a.accttype = 'Income' THEN -tal.amount ELSE 0 END)
        - SUM(CASE WHEN a.accttype = 'COGS' THEN tal.amount ELSE 0 END) AS gross_profit
FROM transactionaccountingline tal
JOIN transaction t ON t.id = tal.transaction
JOIN account a ON a.id = tal.account
JOIN accountingperiod ap ON ap.id = t.postingperiod
WHERE tal.posting = 'T'
    AND ap.isadjust = 'F'
    AND ap.isquarter = 'F'
    AND ap.isyear = 'F'
    AND ap.startdate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND ap.startdate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY TO_CHAR(ap.startdate, 'YYYY') || '-Q' || TO_CHAR(ap.startdate, 'Q')
ORDER BY MIN(ap.startdate)
entries.caveats: Quarter bucket is COMPUTED from month-period startdate (TO_CHAR 'Q'); isquarter='F' AND isyear='F' AND isadjust='F' guarantee only base month periods feed the sums, so rollup rows can never double-count. Signed GL convention: income = credit - debit = SUM(-amount); COGS/expense = debit - credit = SUM(amount). The 'Q' format yields CALENDAR quarters — companies on a shifted fiscal calendar (e.g. Feb start) should map startdate to their fiscal quarter instead. The full concatenation expression is repeated in GROUP BY; ORDER BY uses aggregate MIN(startdate) for chronological order.

entries.scenario: The group tax accountant of a multi-subsidiary products distributor files separate tax returns per legal entity and needs sales tax collected split by subsidiary and period, converted to each subsidiary's base currency.
entries.question: How much sales tax did each subsidiary collect per period, in its base currency?
entries.intent: Sums taxline='T' lines on posted CustInvc/CustCred grouped by transactionline subsidiary and posting period, converting with transaction.exchangerate to subsidiary base currency.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    BUILTIN.DF(tl.subsidiary) AS subsidiary,
    ap.periodname AS period_name,
    SUM(-tl.netamount * t.exchangerate) AS tax_collected_base_currency,
    COUNT(DISTINCT t.id) AS taxed_transactions
FROM transaction t
JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = t.id
JOIN accountingperiod ap ON ap.id = t.postingperiod
WHERE t.posting = 'T'
    AND t.type IN ('CustInvc', 'CustCred')
    AND tl.taxline = 'T'
    AND ap.isadjust = 'F'
    AND ap.startdate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND ap.startdate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY BUILTIN.DF(tl.subsidiary), ap.periodname, ap.startdate
ORDER BY BUILTIN.DF(tl.subsidiary), ap.startdate
entries.caveats: transaction.subsidiary is not queryable, so subsidiary is read from transactionline via BUILTIN.DF. netamount * exchangerate converts each tax line to the TRANSACTION'S OWN subsidiary base — figures are per-entity base currency and must NOT be summed across subsidiaries with different base currencies (grouping by subsidiary keeps them separate, which is what per-entity filing needs). Sales tax lines are credit-signed, hence the negation. BUILTIN.DF expression repeated in GROUP BY (no aliases).

entries.scenario: A staff accountant at a retail wholesaler performs the final review of a single period before locking it and wants posted debit/credit totals broken down by account type as a quick trial-balance-style sanity check.
entries.question: For a given period, what are the posted debits, credits, and net movement by account type?
entries.intent: For one named period (:period), sums posted transactionaccountingline amounts split into debits/credits/net grouped by account.accttype.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    a.accttype AS account_type,
    SUM(CASE WHEN tal.amount > 0 THEN tal.amount ELSE 0 END) AS total_debits,
    SUM(CASE WHEN tal.amount < 0 THEN -tal.amount ELSE 0 END) AS total_credits,
    SUM(tal.amount) AS net_movement
FROM transactionaccountingline tal
JOIN transaction t ON t.id = tal.transaction
JOIN account a ON a.id = tal.account
JOIN accountingperiod ap ON ap.id = t.postingperiod
WHERE tal.posting = 'T'
    AND ap.periodname = :period
    AND ap.isadjust = 'F'
GROUP BY a.accttype
ORDER BY a.accttype
entries.caveats: Uses the GL source of truth (transactionaccountingline, posting='T') rather than header amounts. tal.amount is a single signed column: positives are debits, negatives credits, so the CASE split reconstructs both sides and SUM(net_movement) across all rows should be ~0 for a balanced period. Period selected by name (:period, e.g. 'Jan 2026') with isadjust='F' so a same-named adjustment period is not mixed in. Amounts are subsidiary base currency; totals blend base currencies in multi-currency OneWorld accounts.

entries.scenario: A wholesale distributor's inventory planner reviews the daily stock grid to see how much of each SKU physically sits in each warehouse versus how much is actually promisable to new orders.
entries.question: Show me on-hand vs available quantity for every inventory item by location.
entries.intent: Item-by-location grid of summed on-hand, available, and the committed/held gap from inventorybalance.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    it.itemid AS item_sku,
    it.displayname AS item_name,
    loc.name AS location_name,
    SUM(NVL(ib.quantityonhand, 0)) AS qty_on_hand,
    SUM(NVL(ib.quantityavailable, 0)) AS qty_available,
    SUM(NVL(ib.quantityonhand, 0)) - SUM(NVL(ib.quantityavailable, 0)) AS qty_committed_or_held
FROM inventorybalance ib
JOIN item it ON it.id = ib.item
JOIN location loc ON loc.id = ib.location
WHERE it.itemtype IN ('InvtPart', 'Assembly')
GROUP BY it.itemid, it.displayname, loc.name
ORDER BY it.itemid, loc.name
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: inventorybalance has one row per bin/lot/status slice, so quantities MUST be SUMmed to the item-location grain or the grid shows fragment rows. itemtype is constrained to real stocked products ('InvtPart','Assembly') so kits/non-inventory pseudo-items never appear. No transaction tables involved, so none of the sign/mainline rules apply here.

entries.scenario: A retail products company's buyer runs a morning replenishment alert listing every SKU whose company-wide available quantity has dropped below the reorder trigger so purchase orders can be cut before stockouts.
entries.question: Which items have total available quantity below our reorder threshold across all locations?
entries.intent: Items whose summed available quantity across all locations is below :threshold, with on-hand context and stocked-location count.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    it.itemid AS item_sku,
    it.displayname AS item_name,
    SUM(NVL(ib.quantityonhand, 0)) AS total_on_hand,
    SUM(NVL(ib.quantityavailable, 0)) AS total_available,
    COUNT(DISTINCT ib.location) AS stocked_locations
FROM inventorybalance ib
JOIN item it ON it.id = ib.item
WHERE it.itemtype IN ('InvtPart', 'Assembly')
GROUP BY it.itemid, it.displayname
HAVING SUM(NVL(ib.quantityavailable, 0)) < :threshold
ORDER BY SUM(NVL(ib.quantityavailable, 0)) ASC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: Uses a :threshold parameter instead of item.reorderpoint because per-location reorder points are not reliably exposed via SuiteQL in all accounts. Threshold test lives in HAVING on the summed expression (aggregate filter), and the expression is repeated rather than aliased. NVL guards null quantity slices. Items with no inventorybalance rows at all (never stocked) will not appear — this is a low-stock list, not a full catalog gap list.

entries.scenario: A fulfillment operations manager needs the list of item-location combinations that can no longer promise stock — including cells where units are physically on the shelf but fully committed to open orders, and oversold cells gone negative.
entries.question: Which item-location combinations have zero or negative available quantity right now?
entries.intent: Item-location cells where summed available <= 0, showing on-hand so fully-committed cells are distinguishable from true empties.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    it.itemid AS item_sku,
    it.displayname AS item_name,
    loc.name AS location_name,
    SUM(NVL(ib.quantityonhand, 0)) AS qty_on_hand,
    SUM(NVL(ib.quantityavailable, 0)) AS qty_available
FROM inventorybalance ib
JOIN item it ON it.id = ib.item
JOIN location loc ON loc.id = ib.location
WHERE it.itemtype IN ('InvtPart', 'Assembly')
GROUP BY it.itemid, it.displayname, loc.name
HAVING SUM(NVL(ib.quantityavailable, 0)) <= 0
ORDER BY SUM(NVL(ib.quantityonhand, 0)) DESC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: The <=0 test must run in HAVING on the SUM, not in WHERE on raw rows — a single negative bin slice does not mean the location cell is out. Result mixes two states the reader must distinguish via qty_on_hand: on_hand > 0 means stock exists but is fully committed/held; on_hand = 0 (or negative available) means a true empty or oversold cell. Sorted by on-hand DESC so committed-but-stocked cells surface first for expediting.

entries.scenario: A director of warehouse operations compares the network's sites each week: how many SKUs each warehouse carries, its total unit position, how much is committed, and what share of the company's total units it holds.
entries.question: Give me the total inventory position by location: item count, units on hand, available, committed, and each location's share of company-wide units.
entries.intent: Per-location rollup of distinct items, on-hand, available, committed units, and percent of company-wide on-hand units via a scalar subquery.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    loc.name AS location_name,
    COUNT(DISTINCT ib.item) AS distinct_items,
    SUM(NVL(ib.quantityonhand, 0)) AS units_on_hand,
    SUM(NVL(ib.quantityavailable, 0)) AS units_available,
    SUM(NVL(ib.quantityonhand, 0)) - SUM(NVL(ib.quantityavailable, 0)) AS units_committed,
    ROUND(100 * SUM(NVL(ib.quantityonhand, 0)) /
        (SELECT SUM(NVL(b2.quantityonhand, 0)) FROM inventorybalance b2), 2) AS pct_of_company_units
FROM inventorybalance ib
