JOIN location loc ON loc.id = ib.location
GROUP BY loc.name
ORDER BY SUM(NVL(ib.quantityonhand, 0)) DESC
entries.caveats: Share-of-company denominator is a scalar subquery over all of inventorybalance — it counts raw UNITS across every item, so this is a unit-mix comparison, not a value comparison (a location full of small parts can look dominant). Assumes at least one non-zero on-hand row company-wide (otherwise the divisor is null/zero). Committed is derived as on_hand - available, which also absorbs bin holds, not only sales commitments.

entries.scenario: A supply chain risk analyst flags single-point-of-failure SKUs: items where one warehouse holds an outsized share of the company's stock, so a site disruption would halt fulfillment for those products.
entries.question: Which items have a dangerously high concentration of their stock in a single location?
entries.intent: Item-location cells where one location holds >= :threshold percent of that item's total on-hand units, computed by joining a per-location aggregate to a per-item aggregate.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    it.itemid AS item_sku,
    it.displayname AS item_name,
    loc.name AS location_name,
    locagg.loc_units AS units_at_location,
    itemagg.total_units AS total_units,
    ROUND(100 * locagg.loc_units / itemagg.total_units, 1) AS pct_at_location
FROM (
    SELECT ib.item AS item, ib.location AS location,
        SUM(NVL(ib.quantityonhand, 0)) AS loc_units
    FROM inventorybalance ib
    GROUP BY ib.item, ib.location
) locagg
JOIN (
    SELECT ib2.item AS item, SUM(NVL(ib2.quantityonhand, 0)) AS total_units
    FROM inventorybalance ib2
    GROUP BY ib2.item
    HAVING SUM(NVL(ib2.quantityonhand, 0)) > 0
) itemagg ON itemagg.item = locagg.item
JOIN item it ON it.id = locagg.item
JOIN location loc ON loc.id = locagg.location
WHERE it.itemtype IN ('InvtPart', 'Assembly')
    AND 100 * locagg.loc_units / itemagg.total_units >= :threshold
ORDER BY 100 * locagg.loc_units / itemagg.total_units DESC, itemagg.total_units DESC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: Avoids window functions (not reliably supported in SuiteQL) by joining two derived aggregates on item. The per-item denominator subquery filters HAVING total > 0 to prevent divide-by-zero and to exclude items with only negative/zero balances. The percentage expression is repeated verbatim in WHERE and ORDER BY because aliases cannot be referenced there. :threshold is a percent (e.g. 80).

entries.scenario: An order management lead investigates why warehouse shelves look full while the website shows items unsellable — ranking SKUs by how many on-hand units are already committed to open orders or held.
entries.question: Which items have the most units committed (on hand but not available), and what share of their stock is locked up?
entries.intent: Top items by derived committed quantity (on_hand - available) with percent-committed, filtered to items with positive on-hand and positive commitment.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    it.itemid AS item_sku,
    it.displayname AS item_name,
    SUM(NVL(ib.quantityonhand, 0)) AS qty_on_hand,
    SUM(NVL(ib.quantityavailable, 0)) AS qty_available,
    SUM(NVL(ib.quantityonhand, 0)) - SUM(NVL(ib.quantityavailable, 0)) AS qty_committed,
    ROUND(100 * (SUM(NVL(ib.quantityonhand, 0)) - SUM(NVL(ib.quantityavailable, 0)))
        / SUM(NVL(ib.quantityonhand, 0)), 1) AS pct_committed
FROM inventorybalance ib
JOIN item it ON it.id = ib.item
WHERE it.itemtype IN ('InvtPart', 'Assembly')
GROUP BY it.itemid, it.displayname
HAVING SUM(NVL(ib.quantityonhand, 0)) > 0
    AND SUM(NVL(ib.quantityonhand, 0)) - SUM(NVL(ib.quantityavailable, 0)) > 0
ORDER BY SUM(NVL(ib.quantityonhand, 0)) - SUM(NVL(ib.quantityavailable, 0)) DESC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: Committed is DERIVED as on_hand - available because a dedicated committed column is not consistently exposed on inventorybalance; the difference also includes bin/status holds, so treat it as 'unavailable', not strictly 'sales-committed'. HAVING requires on_hand > 0 to make pct_committed's divisor safe, and committed > 0 to drop fully-free items. All aggregate expressions are repeated in HAVING/ORDER BY (no alias references).

entries.scenario: A merchandising manager preparing a cycle-count and slotting review wants the highest-volume SKUs by physical units across the whole network to prioritize count frequency and prime pick locations.
entries.question: What are our top items by total units on hand across all locations?
entries.intent: Ranking of inventory items by company-wide summed on-hand units, with available units and stocked-location count for context.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    it.itemid AS item_sku,
    it.displayname AS item_name,
    SUM(NVL(ib.quantityonhand, 0)) AS total_units_on_hand,
    SUM(NVL(ib.quantityavailable, 0)) AS total_units_available,
    COUNT(DISTINCT ib.location) AS stocked_locations
FROM inventorybalance ib
JOIN item it ON it.id = ib.item
WHERE it.itemtype IN ('InvtPart', 'Assembly')
GROUP BY it.itemid, it.displayname
ORDER BY SUM(NVL(ib.quantityonhand, 0)) DESC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: Ranking uses FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY (never LIMIT/OFFSET in SuiteQL). Units are summed across bin/lot/status slices before ranking. This is a unit ranking, not a value ranking — item cost is deliberately not joined, so high-count/low-value parts will dominate; that is the correct view for count-frequency planning.

entries.scenario: A warehouse network coordinator hunts for transfer opportunities: SKUs that are out of stock (or oversold) at one site while other sites still hold available units, so stock can be rebalanced instead of purchased.
entries.question: Which items are out of stock at one location but still available at other locations?
entries.intent: Item-location cells with available <= 0 joined to the item's company-wide available total, returning how many units exist elsewhere for potential transfer.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    it.itemid AS item_sku,
    it.displayname AS item_name,
    loc.name AS stockout_location,
    here.avail_here AS available_at_location,
    tot.total_avail - here.avail_here AS available_elsewhere
FROM (
    SELECT ib.item AS item, ib.location AS location,
        SUM(NVL(ib.quantityavailable, 0)) AS avail_here
    FROM inventorybalance ib
    GROUP BY ib.item, ib.location
    HAVING SUM(NVL(ib.quantityavailable, 0)) <= 0
) here
JOIN (
    SELECT ib2.item AS item, SUM(NVL(ib2.quantityavailable, 0)) AS total_avail
    FROM inventorybalance ib2
    GROUP BY ib2.item
) tot ON tot.item = here.item
JOIN item it ON it.id = here.item
JOIN location loc ON loc.id = here.location
WHERE it.itemtype IN ('InvtPart', 'Assembly')
    AND tot.total_avail - here.avail_here > 0
ORDER BY tot.total_avail - here.avail_here DESC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: available_elsewhere is computed as company total minus the stockout cell (total_avail - avail_here) so a negative/oversold cell correctly INCREASES what the rest of the network must cover. Both sides aggregate to the correct grain before joining — joining raw bin-level rows would double count. Only locations where the item has ever had a balance row appear as stockout candidates; locations that never stocked the SKU are absent by design.

entries.scenario: A demand planner at a consumer goods wholesaler blends current availability with trailing 90-day sales velocity to find SKUs whose stock covers fewer days of demand than the replenishment lead time.
entries.question: Which selling items have fewer than a target number of days of available stock based on the last 90 days of sales?
entries.intent: Items sold in the trailing 90 days (invoices + cash sales at line level) joined to current total available, filtered to days-of-cover below :threshold days.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    it.itemid AS item_sku,
    it.displayname AS item_name,
    NVL(inv.available_units, 0) AS qty_available,
    sales.units_sold_90d AS units_sold_90d,
    ROUND(sales.units_sold_90d / 90, 2) AS avg_daily_units,
    ROUND(NVL(inv.available_units, 0) * 90 / sales.units_sold_90d, 1) AS days_of_cover
FROM (
    SELECT tl.item AS item, SUM(-tl.quantity) AS units_sold_90d
    FROM transaction t
    JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = t.id
    WHERE t.type IN ('CustInvc', 'CashSale')
        AND tl.mainline = 'F'
        AND tl.taxline = 'F'
        AND t.trandate >= TRUNC(SYSDATE) - 90
    GROUP BY tl.item
    HAVING SUM(-tl.quantity) > 0
) sales
JOIN item it ON it.id = sales.item
LEFT JOIN (
    SELECT ib.item AS item, SUM(NVL(ib.quantityavailable, 0)) AS available_units
    FROM inventorybalance ib
    GROUP BY ib.item
) inv ON inv.item = sales.item
WHERE it.itemtype IN ('InvtPart', 'Assembly')
    AND NVL(inv.available_units, 0) * 90 < :threshold * sales.units_sold_90d
ORDER BY NVL(inv.available_units, 0) * 90 / sales.units_sold_90d ASC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: Sales line quantities are NEGATIVE in NetSuite, so velocity is SUM(-tl.quantity), and the line aggregate filters mainline='F' AND taxline='F' plus real product itemtypes to exclude Subtotal/Discount/Markup pseudo-lines. Uses CustInvc/CashSale (actual sales), not SalesOrd, so booked-but-unshipped demand is excluded. Days-of-cover filter is written multiplicatively (available*90 < :threshold*units) to avoid division before the HAVING guard; the divisor in SELECT/ORDER BY is safe because HAVING enforces units_sold_90d > 0. LEFT JOIN + NVL keeps items that sold but now have zero balance rows. :threshold is days (e.g. 30).

entries.scenario: A wholesale distributor's demand planner needs a monthly units-shipped history per SKU to feed reorder-point calculations and spot seasonality.
entries.question: How many units of each item did we ship per month between two dates?
entries.intent: Monthly shipped units per item from item fulfillment (ItemShip) lines over a date range.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    itm.itemid AS item_name,
    TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM') AS ship_month,
    SUM(-tl.quantity) AS units_shipped
FROM transaction t
JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = t.id
JOIN item itm ON itm.id = tl.item
WHERE t.type = 'ItemShip'
    AND tl.mainline = 'F'
    AND tl.taxline = 'F'
    AND itm.itemtype IN ('InvtPart', 'Assembly')
    AND t.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND t.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY itm.itemid, TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM')
ORDER BY itm.itemid, TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM')
entries.caveats: Item fulfillment line quantities are NEGATIVE (inventory going out), so shipped units are SUM(-tl.quantity). mainline='F' AND taxline='F' prevents header/tax double counting. itemtype is constrained to InvtPart/Assembly so Subtotal/Discount/Markup pseudo-items and shipping lines never pollute the sum. GROUP BY repeats the full TO_CHAR expression (no aliases in GROUP BY).

entries.scenario: A purchasing manager at a retail products company wants to see which vendors actually delivered the most units of each item, to verify supplier performance against POs.
entries.question: Which items did we receive the most units of, and from which vendors?
entries.intent: Total received units and receipt count per item and vendor from item receipt (ItemRcpt) lines over a date range, ranked by units received.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    itm.itemid AS item_name,
    v.entityid AS vendor_name,
    SUM(tl.quantity) AS units_received,
    COUNT(DISTINCT t.id) AS receipt_count
FROM transaction t
JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = t.id
JOIN item itm ON itm.id = tl.item
JOIN vendor v ON v.id = t.entity
WHERE t.type = 'ItemRcpt'
    AND tl.mainline = 'F'
    AND tl.taxline = 'F'
    AND itm.itemtype IN ('InvtPart', 'Assembly')
    AND t.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND t.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY itm.itemid, v.entityid
ORDER BY SUM(tl.quantity) DESC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: Item receipt line quantities are POSITIVE (inventory coming in), so no negation is needed — unlike ItemShip. entity is polymorphic: joining vendor is correct for PO-sourced receipts, but the INNER JOIN intentionally drops receipts created from transfer orders (no vendor entity); use SC-MOVE-003 for total inbound including transfers. mainline/taxline filters and the itemtype constraint prevent pseudo-line contamination. FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY (never LIMIT/OFFSET).

entries.scenario: An inventory controller wants a single view of physical inflow vs outflow per SKU for the quarter to explain why on-hand levels rose or fell.
entries.question: For each item, how many units did we receive vs ship in a period, and what was the net movement?
entries.intent: Per-item received units, shipped units, and net movement (receipts minus shipments) combining ItemRcpt and ItemShip lines in one pass.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    itm.itemid AS item_name,
    SUM(CASE WHEN t.type = 'ItemRcpt' THEN tl.quantity ELSE 0 END) AS units_received,
    SUM(CASE WHEN t.type = 'ItemShip' THEN -tl.quantity ELSE 0 END) AS units_shipped,
    SUM(CASE WHEN t.type = 'ItemRcpt' THEN tl.quantity ELSE 0 END)
        - SUM(CASE WHEN t.type = 'ItemShip' THEN -tl.quantity ELSE 0 END) AS net_movement
FROM transaction t
JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = t.id
JOIN item itm ON itm.id = tl.item
WHERE t.type IN ('ItemRcpt', 'ItemShip')
    AND tl.mainline = 'F'
    AND tl.taxline = 'F'
    AND itm.itemtype IN ('InvtPart', 'Assembly')
    AND t.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND t.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY itm.itemid
ORDER BY SUM(CASE WHEN t.type = 'ItemRcpt' THEN tl.quantity ELSE 0 END)
    - SUM(CASE WHEN t.type = 'ItemShip' THEN -tl.quantity ELSE 0 END) DESC
entries.caveats: Sign conventions differ by type: ItemRcpt line quantity is positive, ItemShip line quantity is negative — the CASE branches normalize both to positive columns before computing net. ORDER BY repeats the full aggregate expression rather than relying on an alias. Includes transfer-order-sourced receipts and fulfillments, so net movement reflects ALL physical flow, not just buy/sell. mainline='F' AND taxline='F' plus the itemtype filter keep pseudo-lines out.

entries.scenario: A distribution operations lead needs monthly inbound/outbound unit volume per warehouse to balance labor staffing and identify which sites carry the throughput.
entries.question: Show monthly units shipped and received by warehouse location.
entries.intent: Monthly shipped and received units grouped by transactionline location across ItemShip and ItemRcpt transactions.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    BUILTIN.DF(tl.location) AS location_name,
    TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM') AS move_month,
    SUM(CASE WHEN t.type = 'ItemShip' THEN -tl.quantity ELSE 0 END) AS units_shipped,
    SUM(CASE WHEN t.type = 'ItemRcpt' THEN tl.quantity ELSE 0 END) AS units_received
FROM transaction t
JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = t.id
JOIN item itm ON itm.id = tl.item
WHERE t.type IN ('ItemShip', 'ItemRcpt')
    AND tl.mainline = 'F'
    AND tl.taxline = 'F'
    AND tl.location IS NOT NULL
    AND itm.itemtype IN ('InvtPart', 'Assembly')
    AND t.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND t.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY BUILTIN.DF(tl.location), TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM')
ORDER BY BUILTIN.DF(tl.location), TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM')
entries.caveats: Location lives on transactionline, NOT the transaction header — grouping is on tl.location with BUILTIN.DF for the display name. ItemShip quantities are negated to positive; ItemRcpt quantities are already positive. Lines with NULL location (e.g. non-inventory charges) are excluded explicitly. GROUP BY repeats both full expressions (BUILTIN.DF and TO_CHAR) since aliases are not allowed in GROUP BY.

entries.scenario: A merchandising manager wants a staleness ranking: for every active SKU, when did it last physically move in or out, so the oldest untouched items can be reviewed for markdown.
entries.question: For each item, when was the last shipment and last receipt, and how many days since any movement?
entries.intent: Per-item last ItemShip date, last ItemRcpt date, and days elapsed since the most recent physical movement, ranked stalest first.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    itm.itemid AS item_name,
    MAX(CASE WHEN t.type = 'ItemShip' THEN t.trandate END) AS last_ship_date,
    MAX(CASE WHEN t.type = 'ItemRcpt' THEN t.trandate END) AS last_receipt_date,
    TRUNC(SYSDATE) - MAX(t.trandate) AS days_since_any_movement
FROM transaction t
JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = t.id
JOIN item itm ON itm.id = tl.item
WHERE t.type IN ('ItemShip', 'ItemRcpt')
    AND tl.mainline = 'F'
    AND tl.taxline = 'F'
    AND itm.itemtype IN ('InvtPart', 'Assembly')
GROUP BY itm.itemid
ORDER BY TRUNC(SYSDATE) - MAX(t.trandate) DESC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: This is a movement-date ageing PROXY: without the inventorynumber/lot tables, true FIFO layer age is not queryable, so days-since-last-movement stands in for it. Oracle date subtraction (TRUNC(SYSDATE) - MAX(trandate)) yields days as a number. Conditional MAX(CASE...) splits ship vs receipt recency in one pass; items with movement of only one type show NULL for the other. Items that have NEVER moved do not appear at all — pair with SC-MOVE-006 to catch those. ORDER BY repeats the expression, no alias.

entries.scenario: A finance and supply chain team preparing an obsolescence reserve needs the list of items still sitting in the warehouse that have not shipped at all since a cutoff date.
entries.question: Which items have stock on hand but zero shipments since a given date?
entries.intent: Items with positive summed on-hand quantity in inventorybalance and NO ItemShip lines on or after :start, via NOT EXISTS.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    itm.itemid AS item_name,
    itm.displayname AS item_description,
    SUM(ib.quantityonhand) AS on_hand_units
FROM inventorybalance ib
JOIN item itm ON itm.id = ib.item
WHERE itm.itemtype IN ('InvtPart', 'Assembly')
    AND NOT EXISTS (
        SELECT 1
        FROM transactionline tl
        JOIN transaction t ON t.id = tl.transaction
        WHERE tl.item = ib.item
            AND t.type = 'ItemShip'
            AND tl.mainline = 'F'
            AND tl.taxline = 'F'
            AND t.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    )
GROUP BY itm.itemid, itm.displayname
HAVING SUM(ib.quantityonhand) > 0
ORDER BY SUM(ib.quantityonhand) DESC
entries.caveats: inventorybalance is grained by location/status, so quantityonhand is SUMmed per item; HAVING keeps only items with net positive stock. NOT EXISTS is used instead of a LEFT JOIN anti-pattern so one shipment line anywhere disqualifies the item cleanly and the subquery still enforces mainline='F'/taxline='F'. Set :start to your staleness cutoff (e.g. 6 or 12 months back). Companies that ship via CashSale without fulfillments should add that type to the subquery.

entries.scenario: An inventory planner needs a slow-mover ranking expressed as months of cover — how many months current stock would last at the trailing-12-month shipment rate — to prioritize purchasing freezes and clearance.
entries.question: Rank items by months of stock cover: on-hand units divided by average monthly units shipped over the last 12 months.
entries.intent: Per-item on-hand units, trailing-12-month shipped units, and months-of-cover ratio; items with stock but zero shipments sort to the top.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    itm.itemid AS item_name,
    oh.on_hand_units AS on_hand_units,
    NVL(sh.units_shipped_12m, 0) AS units_shipped_12m,
    CASE WHEN NVL(sh.units_shipped_12m, 0) <= 0 THEN NULL
        ELSE ROUND(oh.on_hand_units / (sh.units_shipped_12m / 12), 1) END AS months_of_cover
FROM item itm
JOIN (
    SELECT ib.item AS item, SUM(ib.quantityonhand) AS on_hand_units
    FROM inventorybalance ib
    GROUP BY ib.item
) oh ON oh.item = itm.id
LEFT JOIN (
    SELECT tl.item AS item, SUM(-tl.quantity) AS units_shipped_12m
    FROM transaction t
    JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = t.id
    WHERE t.type = 'ItemShip'
        AND tl.mainline = 'F'
        AND tl.taxline = 'F'
        AND t.trandate >= ADD_MONTHS(TRUNC(SYSDATE), -12)
    GROUP BY tl.item
) sh ON sh.item = itm.id
WHERE itm.itemtype IN ('InvtPart', 'Assembly')
    AND oh.on_hand_units > 0
ORDER BY CASE WHEN NVL(sh.units_shipped_12m, 0) <= 0 THEN 999999
    ELSE oh.on_hand_units / (sh.units_shipped_12m / 12) END DESC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: Both sides are pre-aggregated in subqueries before joining, so the many-to-many between inventorybalance rows and shipment lines can never multiply units. Shipments use SUM(-tl.quantity) because ItemShip line quantities are negative. LEFT JOIN keeps never-shipped items; the ORDER BY CASE sentinel (999999) pushes zero-velocity items with stock to the top of the slow-mover list while months_of_cover shows NULL (undefined, not infinite). Trailing window is anchored with ADD_MONTHS(TRUNC(SYSDATE), -12).

entries.scenario: A controller wants item movement valued in dollars, not just units: the monthly COGS the GL actually recorded when fulfillments shipped, by item and subsidiary, to reconcile ops throughput to the P&L.
entries.question: What COGS value posted to the GL from item fulfillments, by item and month?
entries.intent: Monthly GL-posted COGS amount per item and subsidiary from transactionaccountingline rows on ItemShip transactions hitting COGS-type accounts.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    itm.itemid AS item_name,
    BUILTIN.DF(tl.subsidiary) AS subsidiary_name,
    TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM') AS ship_month,
    SUM(tal.amount) AS cogs_posted
FROM transaction t
JOIN transactionaccountingline tal ON tal.transaction = t.id
JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = tal.transaction
    AND tl.id = tal.transactionline
JOIN account a ON a.id = tal.account
JOIN item itm ON itm.id = tl.item
WHERE t.type = 'ItemShip'
    AND tal.posting = 'T'
    AND a.accttype = 'COGS'
    AND itm.itemtype IN ('InvtPart', 'Assembly')
    AND t.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND t.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY itm.itemid, BUILTIN.DF(tl.subsidiary), TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM')
ORDER BY SUM(tal.amount) DESC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: GL grain, not line grain: truth comes from transactionaccountingline with posting='T'; tal.amount is signed debit-positive, and COGS is an expense (debit - credit), so SUM(tal.amount) is directly the positive COGS posted — no negation. The compound join on (transaction, transactionline) ties each GL impact back to its item line for the item attribution. Subsidiary comes from transactionline (header subsidiary is not queryable) and results are in each subsidiary's base currency — do NOT sum across subsidiaries without translation. In accounts configured to post COGS at invoicing instead of fulfillment, change t.type to 'CustInvc'.

entries.scenario: A purchasing manager at a wholesale distribution company wants a monthly view of committed purchase spend per vendor to track ordering trends and spot vendors whose order volume is ramping up or falling off.
entries.question: Show me total purchase order value and PO count by vendor and month for a date range.
entries.intent: Aggregates PurchOrd header totals and counts per vendor per calendar month over a parameterized date range.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    v.entityid AS vendor,
    TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM') AS po_month,
    COUNT(*) AS po_count,
    SUM(t.foreigntotal) AS po_total
FROM transaction t
    JOIN vendor v ON v.id = t.entity
WHERE t.type = 'PurchOrd'
    AND t.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND t.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY v.entityid, TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM')
ORDER BY v.entityid, TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM')
entries.caveats: PurchOrd headers store foreigntotal as a positive amount in the transaction currency (unlike vendor bills, which are credit-signed negative), so no negation is needed here. foreigntotal is NOT base currency: mixed-currency vendors sum nominal amounts; multiply by transaction.exchangerate if base-currency consolidation is needed. Entity is joined to vendor because PurchOrd is an AP-side type. GROUP BY repeats the TO_CHAR expression (no aliases allowed in GROUP BY).

entries.scenario: A supply chain director at a consumer-products importer reviews the largest purchase commitments each quarter before cash-flow planning meetings and needs the biggest POs with their current fulfillment status.
entries.question: What are our largest purchase orders by amount in this date range, with vendor and current status?
entries.intent: Ranks PurchOrd headers by foreigntotal descending with vendor, display status, and currency, limited to top :n rows.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    t.tranid AS po_number,
    t.trandate AS po_date,
    v.entityid AS vendor,
    BUILTIN.DF(t.status) AS status,
    BUILTIN.DF(t.currency) AS currency,
    t.foreigntotal AS po_total
FROM transaction t
    JOIN vendor v ON v.id = t.entity
WHERE t.type = 'PurchOrd'
    AND t.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND t.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
ORDER BY t.foreigntotal DESC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: Status is displayed with BUILTIN.DF(t.status) because transaction.status is a raw per-type letter code ('A','B',...) that must never be compared to words like 'open'. foreigntotal is the transaction-currency amount, so currency is displayed alongside to avoid misreading mixed-currency rankings. Uses FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY (never LIMIT/OFFSET).

entries.scenario: A procurement analyst at a retail products company is consolidating the supplier base and wants to see which vendors receive many small POs versus few large ones, to identify candidates for blanket orders.
entries.question: Give me PO count, total PO value, and average PO value by vendor for a date range, ranked by total spend.
entries.intent: Per-vendor PurchOrd count, sum, average, and max of header totals over a date range, top :n vendors by total.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    v.entityid AS vendor,
    COUNT(*) AS po_count,
    SUM(t.foreigntotal) AS total_po_value,
    ROUND(AVG(t.foreigntotal), 2) AS avg_po_value,
    MAX(t.foreigntotal) AS largest_po
FROM transaction t
    JOIN vendor v ON v.id = t.entity
WHERE t.type = 'PurchOrd'
    AND t.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND t.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY v.entityid
ORDER BY SUM(t.foreigntotal) DESC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: Header-grain query: no transactionline join is needed, so no mainline filter applies. PurchOrd foreigntotal is positive (vendor-bill negative-sign rule does not apply to POs). Averages mix transaction currencies nominally for multi-currency vendors. ORDER BY repeats the aggregate expression rather than the alias for maximum dialect safety.

entries.scenario: A buyer at a multi-subsidiary hardgoods wholesaler runs a weekly open-PO review to chase overdue inbound orders and needs every PO still awaiting receipt or billing, with its age and owning subsidiary.
entries.question: List all open purchase orders (not yet fully received/billed) with status, subsidiary, amount, and age in days.
entries.intent: Lists PurchOrd headers in open raw status codes (B, D, E, F) with BUILTIN.DF status/subsidiary display and age from trandate.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    t.tranid AS po_number,
    t.trandate AS po_date,
    v.entityid AS vendor,
    BUILTIN.DF(t.status) AS status,
    BUILTIN.DF(tl.subsidiary) AS subsidiary,
    t.foreigntotal AS po_total,
    TRUNC(SYSDATE) - t.trandate AS age_days
FROM transaction t
    JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = t.id AND tl.mainline = 'T'
    JOIN vendor v ON v.id = t.entity
WHERE t.type = 'PurchOrd'
    AND t.status IN ('B', 'D', 'E', 'F')
ORDER BY t.trandate
entries.caveats: transaction.status is a RAW per-type letter code, so 'open' is expressed as the PurchOrd codes B (Pending Receipt), D (Partially Received), E (Pending Billing/Partially Received), F (Pending Bill) — never status='open'. The human-readable label comes from BUILTIN.DF(t.status). transaction.subsidiary is not queryable, so subsidiary is read from the mainline='T' transactionline row via BUILTIN.DF(tl.subsidiary); mainline='T' guarantees exactly one line per PO so the header grain is preserved.

entries.scenario: An inbound logistics coordinator at a distribution company tracks how much physical product each supplier actually delivered per month, to compare receiving workload and supplier throughput against plan.
entries.question: How many item receipts and how many units did we receive from each vendor per month?
entries.intent: Counts ItemRcpt transactions and sums received line quantity per vendor per month, restricted to real product item types.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    v.entityid AS vendor,
    TO_CHAR(r.trandate, 'YYYY-MM') AS receipt_month,
    COUNT(DISTINCT r.id) AS receipt_count,
    SUM(rl.quantity) AS units_received
FROM transaction r
    JOIN transactionline rl ON rl.transaction = r.id AND rl.mainline = 'F' AND rl.taxline = 'F'
    JOIN item i ON i.id = rl.item
    JOIN vendor v ON v.id = r.entity
WHERE r.type = 'ItemRcpt'
    AND i.itemtype IN ('InvtPart', 'Assembly', 'NonInvtPart')
    AND r.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND r.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY v.entityid, TO_CHAR(r.trandate, 'YYYY-MM')
ORDER BY v.entityid, TO_CHAR(r.trandate, 'YYYY-MM')
entries.caveats: Line-grain sum, so mainline='F' AND taxline='F' are mandatory to avoid double counting header/tax rows, and item.itemtype is constrained to real product types so Subtotal/Markup/Discount pseudo-items never pollute quantity. Item receipt line quantities are positive (unlike sales lines, which would need negation). COUNT(DISTINCT r.id) counts receipts, not lines, so multi-line receipts are not overcounted. Entity on ItemRcpt resolves to the vendor from the source PO.

entries.scenario: A finance-facing procurement lead at a wholesale company wants a per-vendor comparison of what was ordered on POs versus what was actually billed in the same window, as a rough proxy for PO-to-bill conversion and un-billed commitment.
entries.question: For each vendor, compare total PO value versus total vendor bill value in a date range and compute the bill-to-PO ratio.
entries.intent: Conditional aggregation over PurchOrd and VendBill headers per vendor: PO total, sign-corrected bill total, and their ratio.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    v.entityid AS vendor,
    SUM(CASE WHEN t.type = 'PurchOrd' THEN t.foreigntotal ELSE 0 END) AS po_total,
    SUM(CASE WHEN t.type = 'VendBill' THEN -t.foreigntotal ELSE 0 END) AS billed_total,
    CASE
        WHEN SUM(CASE WHEN t.type = 'PurchOrd' THEN t.foreigntotal ELSE 0 END) = 0 THEN NULL
        ELSE ROUND(
            SUM(CASE WHEN t.type = 'VendBill' THEN -t.foreigntotal ELSE 0 END)
            / SUM(CASE WHEN t.type = 'PurchOrd' THEN t.foreigntotal ELSE 0 END), 4)
    END AS bill_to_po_ratio
FROM transaction t
    JOIN vendor v ON v.id = t.entity
WHERE t.type IN ('PurchOrd', 'VendBill')
    AND t.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND t.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY v.entityid
ORDER BY SUM(CASE WHEN t.type = 'PurchOrd' THEN t.foreigntotal ELSE 0 END) DESC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: Vendor bill foreigntotal is CREDIT-SIGNED (negative), so it is negated to positive spend, while PurchOrd foreigntotal is already positive — mixing them without the sign fix silently cancels the totals. The ratio is a period proxy, not a document-level link: bills dated in the window may bill POs from an earlier window, so ratios above 1 or below 1 near period boundaries are expected. Division is guarded with CASE (no alias references, expressions repeated).

entries.scenario: A controller at a products company auditing procurement wants GL-grade truth on where each vendor's bills actually posted in a given period — inventory, COGS, expense, or fixed assets — to catch miscoded supplier invoices.
entries.question: For a given accounting period, show posted vendor bill amounts by vendor and account type from the GL.
entries.intent: Sums posting='T' transactionaccountingline amounts on VendBill by vendor and account.accttype for one accounting period, excluding the AP offset.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    v.entityid AS vendor,
    a.accttype AS account_type,
    SUM(tal.amount) AS posted_amount
FROM transaction t
    JOIN transactionaccountingline tal ON tal.transaction = t.id
    JOIN account a ON a.id = tal.account
    JOIN vendor v ON v.id = t.entity
    JOIN accountingperiod ap ON ap.id = t.postingperiod
WHERE t.type = 'VendBill'
    AND tal.posting = 'T'
    AND a.accttype <> 'AcctPay'
    AND ap.periodname = :period
    AND ap.isadjust = 'F'
GROUP BY v.entityid, a.accttype
ORDER BY SUM(tal.amount) DESC
entries.caveats: GL truth comes from transactionaccountingline with posting='T'; tal.amount is signed (debit +, credit -), so the debit-side lines of a bill (Expense/COGS/InvAsset-type accounts) come out positive naturally and the AP credit offset is excluded via accttype <> 'AcctPay' instead of sign games. Period filtering joins accountingperiod through transaction.postingperiod and requires isadjust='F' to avoid rollup/adjustment rows. Amounts are in the transaction's subsidiary base ledger context.

entries.scenario: A supply chain planner at an importer is validating vendor lead-time assumptions in the planning system and needs the actual average days from PO date to item receipt date for each supplier.
entries.question: What is the average number of days from purchase order date to item receipt date, by vendor?
entries.intent: Links ItemRcpt lines back to their source PurchOrd via transactionline.createdfrom, dedupes to receipt/PO pairs, and averages trandate deltas per vendor.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    v.entityid AS vendor,
    COUNT(*) AS receipt_events,
    ROUND(AVG(x.lead_days), 1) AS avg_lead_days,
    MAX(x.lead_days) AS worst_lead_days
FROM (
    SELECT DISTINCT
        r.id AS receipt_id,
        p.id AS po_id,
        p.entity AS vendor_id,
        (r.trandate - p.trandate) AS lead_days
    FROM transaction r
        JOIN transactionline rl ON rl.transaction = r.id AND rl.mainline = 'F'
        JOIN transaction p ON p.id = rl.createdfrom AND p.type = 'PurchOrd'
    WHERE r.type = 'ItemRcpt'
        AND r.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        AND r.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
) x
    JOIN vendor v ON v.id = x.vendor_id
GROUP BY v.entityid
ORDER BY ROUND(AVG(x.lead_days), 1) DESC
entries.caveats: The receipt-to-PO link lives on transactionline.createdfrom (not the header), so the join goes through non-mainline receipt lines and the inner SELECT DISTINCT collapses back to unique receipt/PO pairs — without it, multi-line receipts would overweight the average. Date subtraction yields days directly in this dialect. This measures PO date to receipt date, which includes vendor processing plus transit; POs never received are absent, so chronic non-shippers must be caught with the open-PO query instead. Vendor is taken from the PO header entity.

entries.scenario: A category manager at a wholesale products company wants to know which items dominate purchasing spend and volume on POs in a period, to prioritize cost negotiations on the highest-spend SKUs.
entries.question: Which items have the highest purchase order spend and ordered quantity in this date range?
entries.intent: Line-grain aggregation of PurchOrd item lines: PO count, ordered quantity, and line amount per item, top :n by spend.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    i.itemid AS item,
    i.itemtype AS item_type,
    COUNT(DISTINCT t.id) AS po_count,
    SUM(tl.quantity) AS qty_ordered,
    SUM(tl.netamount) AS po_line_amount
FROM transaction t
    JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = t.id AND tl.mainline = 'F' AND tl.taxline = 'F'
    JOIN item i ON i.id = tl.item
WHERE t.type = 'PurchOrd'
    AND i.itemtype IN ('InvtPart', 'Assembly', 'NonInvtPart', 'Service')
    AND t.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND t.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY i.itemid, i.itemtype
ORDER BY SUM(tl.netamount) DESC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: Purchase-side line netamount and quantity are POSITIVE (only sales lines are negative and need SUM(-tl.netamount)), so no negation here. mainline='F' AND taxline='F' are required for any line-level sum, and item.itemtype is restricted to real purchasable types so Subtotal/Markup/Discount pseudo-items and description lines are excluded. Amounts are transaction-currency; group by BUILTIN.DF(tl.subsidiary) and apply exchangerate if base-currency consolidation is needed.

entries.scenario: A wholesale distributor's customer service team needs a live worklist of every sales order that is still open so they can answer 'where is my order?' calls without opening each record.
entries.question: Show me all open sales orders with their status, customer, date and total.
entries.intent: Lists open sales order headers (pending approval through pending billing) with a human-readable status via BUILTIN.DF, customer name, date and order total.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    t.tranid AS order_number,
    t.trandate AS order_date,
    c.entityid AS customer,
    BUILTIN.DF(t.status) AS order_status,
    t.foreigntotal AS order_total,
    BUILTIN.DF(t.currency) AS currency
FROM transaction t
JOIN customer c ON c.id = t.entity
WHERE t.type = 'SalesOrd'
    AND t.status IN ('A', 'B', 'D', 'E', 'F')
ORDER BY t.trandate ASC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: transaction.status is a raw letter code per type: for SalesOrd A=Pending Approval, B=Pending Fulfillment, D=Partially Fulfilled, E=Pending Billing/Partially Fulfilled, F=Pending Billing (C/G/H are Cancelled/Billed/Closed) — never filter status='open'. BUILTIN.DF(t.status) is used only for display. foreigntotal is fine here because a sales order is non-posting (the foreignamountunpaid rule applies to AR/AP balances). transaction.subsidiary is not queryable, so subsidiary is intentionally omitted from this header-grain query; join transactionline if you need it.

entries.scenario: The sales operations manager at a consumer products company tracks bookings momentum and needs monthly sales order value per subsidiary to compare against the demand plan.
entries.question: What is our booked sales order value by month and subsidiary for a date range?
entries.intent: Sums sales order line value (sign-corrected, converted to subsidiary base currency via exchangerate) grouped by order month and line subsidiary, excluding pseudo-items.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM') AS order_month,
    BUILTIN.DF(tl.subsidiary) AS subsidiary,
    COUNT(DISTINCT t.id) AS order_count,
    SUM(-tl.netamount * t.exchangerate) AS booked_value_base
FROM transaction t
JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = t.id
JOIN item i ON i.id = tl.item
WHERE t.type = 'SalesOrd'
    AND tl.mainline = 'F'
    AND tl.taxline = 'F'
    AND i.itemtype NOT IN ('Subtotal', 'Markup', 'Discount', 'Description', 'Payment')
    AND t.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND t.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM'), BUILTIN.DF(tl.subsidiary)
ORDER BY TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM'), BUILTIN.DF(tl.subsidiary)
entries.caveats: Sales line netamount is negative, so SUM(-tl.netamount) yields positive bookings. mainline='F' AND taxline='F' prevents double counting the header row and tax lines; the item join excludes Subtotal/Markup/Discount pseudo-items. Subsidiary comes from transactionline (header subsidiary is not queryable). exchangerate converts each order to its own subsidiary's base currency — values are comparable within a subsidiary row but cross-subsidiary totals mix base currencies. GROUP BY repeats the full expressions (no aliases).

entries.scenario: A retail products company's supply planner wants to know which SKUs have shipped fewer units than customers ordered in the period, as an early proxy for backorder exposure before running MRP.
entries.question: For each item, how many units were ordered on sales orders versus shipped on item fulfillments in a date range, and what is the unfulfilled gap?
entries.intent: Conditional aggregation over SalesOrd and ItemShip lines per item: ordered units minus shipped units, returning only items with a positive unfulfilled gap.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    i.itemid AS item,
    i.itemtype AS item_type,
    SUM(CASE WHEN t.type = 'SalesOrd' THEN -tl.quantity ELSE 0 END) AS ordered_units,
    SUM(CASE WHEN t.type = 'ItemShip' THEN -tl.quantity ELSE 0 END) AS shipped_units,
    SUM(CASE WHEN t.type = 'SalesOrd' THEN -tl.quantity ELSE 0 END)
        - SUM(CASE WHEN t.type = 'ItemShip' THEN -tl.quantity ELSE 0 END) AS unfulfilled_units_proxy
FROM transaction t
JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = t.id
JOIN item i ON i.id = tl.item
WHERE t.type IN ('SalesOrd', 'ItemShip')
    AND tl.mainline = 'F'
    AND tl.taxline = 'F'
    AND i.itemtype IN ('InvtPart', 'Assembly', 'Kit')
    AND t.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND t.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY i.itemid, i.itemtype
HAVING SUM(CASE WHEN t.type = 'SalesOrd' THEN -tl.quantity ELSE 0 END)
    - SUM(CASE WHEN t.type = 'ItemShip' THEN -tl.quantity ELSE 0 END) > 0
ORDER BY SUM(CASE WHEN t.type = 'SalesOrd' THEN -tl.quantity ELSE 0 END)
    - SUM(CASE WHEN t.type = 'ItemShip' THEN -tl.quantity ELSE 0 END) DESC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: Both SalesOrd and ItemShip inventory lines carry NEGATIVE transactionline.quantity (outbound convention), so both sides are negated to positive units. This is a period PROXY: fulfillments shipped in-window against orders booked before :start inflate shipped_units, and it does not link SO lines to their specific fulfillments (createdfrom-level tracing is out of scope). mainline='F'/taxline='F' plus restricting itemtype to real stocked types keeps pseudo-lines out. HAVING and ORDER BY repeat the full expressions rather than aliases for dialect safety.

entries.scenario: The warehouse manager at a distribution company runs a daily stand-up on the oldest orders still awaiting fulfillment so the pick team can clear aged demand first.
entries.question: Which are our oldest sales orders still waiting to be fulfilled, and how many days have they been open?
entries.intent: Ranks sales orders in fulfillment-pending statuses by age in days (oldest first) with customer, status display and order total.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    t.tranid AS order_number,
    t.trandate AS order_date,
    TRUNC(SYSDATE) - t.trandate AS days_open,
    c.entityid AS customer,
    BUILTIN.DF(t.status) AS order_status,
    t.foreigntotal AS order_total
FROM transaction t
JOIN customer c ON c.id = t.entity
WHERE t.type = 'SalesOrd'
    AND t.status IN ('B', 'D', 'E')
ORDER BY t.trandate ASC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: Filters the raw SalesOrd letter codes that still have fulfillment outstanding: B=Pending Fulfillment, D=Partially Fulfilled, E=Pending Billing/Partially Fulfilled. Deliberately excludes A (Pending Approval — not releasable to the warehouse) and F (Pending Billing — fully shipped). Never filter status by display label; BUILTIN.DF is display-only. Age computed as TRUNC(SYSDATE) - trandate in the SuiteQL date dialect.

entries.scenario: A multi-warehouse retailer's operations director compares throughput across distribution centers each month to balance labor and decide where to route new orders.
entries.question: How many item fulfillments and shipped units did each location process per month?
entries.intent: Counts item fulfillment transactions and sums shipped units grouped by fulfillment line location and ship month over a date range.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM') AS ship_month,
    BUILTIN.DF(tl.location) AS location,
    COUNT(DISTINCT t.id) AS fulfillment_count,
    SUM(-tl.quantity) AS units_shipped
FROM transaction t
JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = t.id
JOIN item i ON i.id = tl.item
WHERE t.type = 'ItemShip'
    AND tl.mainline = 'F'
    AND tl.taxline = 'F'
    AND i.itemtype IN ('InvtPart', 'Assembly', 'Kit')
    AND t.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND t.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM'), BUILTIN.DF(tl.location)
ORDER BY TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM'), BUILTIN.DF(tl.location)
entries.caveats: Item fulfillments are type 'ItemShip'. Location lives on transactionline, not the transaction header, hence BUILTIN.DF(tl.location). Outbound fulfillment line quantities are negative, so SUM(-tl.quantity) gives positive shipped units. Restricting itemtype to stocked types keeps shipping-charge/service lines out of the unit count. Counts every shipped fulfillment regardless of eventual invoicing status.

entries.scenario: The inventory planner at a wholesale company needs a daily list of SKUs where open sales order demand already exceeds what is available on hand, so purchasing can expedite before customers are put on backorder.
entries.question: Which items have more open sales order units than quantity available in inventory right now?
entries.intent: Aggregates open SO line units per item and left-joins aggregated inventorybalance quantityavailable, returning items where demand exceeds availability ranked by shortfall.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    i.itemid AS item,
    d.open_so_units AS open_so_units,
    NVL(b.qty_available, 0) AS qty_available,
    d.open_so_units - NVL(b.qty_available, 0) AS shortfall_units
FROM (
    SELECT
        tl.item AS item,
        SUM(-tl.quantity) AS open_so_units
    FROM transaction t
    JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = t.id
    WHERE t.type = 'SalesOrd'
        AND t.status IN ('B', 'D', 'E')
        AND tl.mainline = 'F'
        AND tl.taxline = 'F'
        AND tl.isclosed = 'F'
    GROUP BY tl.item
) d
JOIN item i ON i.id = d.item
LEFT JOIN (
    SELECT
        ib.item AS item,
        SUM(ib.quantityavailable) AS qty_available
    FROM inventorybalance ib
    GROUP BY ib.item
) b ON b.item = d.item
WHERE i.itemtype IN ('InvtPart', 'Assembly')
    AND d.open_so_units - NVL(b.qty_available, 0) > :threshold
ORDER BY d.open_so_units - NVL(b.qty_available, 0) DESC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: Demand side uses raw open-status letter codes B/D/E and negates the negative SO line quantity; tl.isclosed='F' drops manually closed lines. Demand is GROSS ordered units on open orders — on partially fulfilled orders the already-shipped portion is still counted (pair with SC-SO-003 for a shipped-net view). inventorybalance is summed across all locations/bins into a company-wide available figure; LEFT JOIN + NVL keeps items with zero inventory rows in the result. :threshold=0 lists every at-risk item.

entries.scenario: A products company's VP of Sales wants to know which customers are carrying the largest unshipped order backlog so account managers can proactively communicate delays to the biggest-impact accounts.
entries.question: Which customers have the highest open (unshipped) sales order backlog value?
entries.intent: Sums sign-corrected open SO line value in subsidiary base currency per customer across fulfillment-pending orders, ranked descending.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    c.entityid AS customer,
    COUNT(DISTINCT t.id) AS open_orders,
    SUM(-tl.netamount * t.exchangerate) AS open_backlog_base
FROM transaction t
JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = t.id
JOIN customer c ON c.id = t.entity
JOIN item i ON i.id = tl.item
WHERE t.type = 'SalesOrd'
    AND t.status IN ('B', 'D', 'E')
    AND tl.mainline = 'F'
    AND tl.taxline = 'F'
    AND tl.isclosed = 'F'
    AND i.itemtype NOT IN ('Subtotal', 'Markup', 'Discount', 'Description', 'Payment')
GROUP BY c.entityid
ORDER BY SUM(-tl.netamount * t.exchangerate) DESC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: SalesOrd is an AR-side type, so entity joins to customer (polymorphic entity rule). Line netamount is negative — negated for positive backlog. Backlog is the full remaining-open line value on orders in statuses B/D/E; on partially fulfilled orders the shipped-but-unbilled portion is still included, so treat as an upper-bound backlog. exchangerate converts to each transaction's subsidiary base currency; if you run multiple base currencies, add BUILTIN.DF(tl.subsidiary) to the grouping before comparing across subsidiaries.

entries.scenario: The supply chain director at a distribution company reviews a weekly aging snapshot of the unshipped order backlog to see whether delayed orders are piling up in the oldest buckets.
entries.question: Break down our open unshipped sales order backlog into aging buckets (0-7, 8-14, 15-30, 31+ days) with order counts and value.
entries.intent: Buckets fulfillment-pending sales orders by days since order date and returns order count and sign-corrected base-currency backlog value per bucket.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    CASE
        WHEN TRUNC(SYSDATE) - t.trandate <= 7 THEN '0-7 days'
        WHEN TRUNC(SYSDATE) - t.trandate <= 14 THEN '8-14 days'
        WHEN TRUNC(SYSDATE) - t.trandate <= 30 THEN '15-30 days'
        ELSE '31+ days'
    END AS age_bucket,
    COUNT(DISTINCT t.id) AS open_orders,
    SUM(-tl.netamount * t.exchangerate) AS backlog_value_base
FROM transaction t
JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = t.id
JOIN item i ON i.id = tl.item
WHERE t.type = 'SalesOrd'
    AND t.status IN ('B', 'D', 'E')
    AND tl.mainline = 'F'
    AND tl.taxline = 'F'
    AND tl.isclosed = 'F'
    AND i.itemtype NOT IN ('Subtotal', 'Markup', 'Discount', 'Description', 'Payment')
GROUP BY CASE
        WHEN TRUNC(SYSDATE) - t.trandate <= 7 THEN '0-7 days'
        WHEN TRUNC(SYSDATE) - t.trandate <= 14 THEN '8-14 days'
        WHEN TRUNC(SYSDATE) - t.trandate <= 30 THEN '15-30 days'
        ELSE '31+ days'
    END
ORDER BY MIN(TRUNC(SYSDATE) - t.trandate)
entries.caveats: The full CASE expression is repeated verbatim in GROUP BY because SuiteQL rejects aliases there; buckets are ordered chronologically via ORDER BY MIN(age) instead of alphabetical bucket labels. Open backlog uses raw status codes B/D/E and open lines only (isclosed='F'), with negated netamount and exchangerate conversion to subsidiary base. Ages from trandate, not promised ship date — if you track expected ship dates in a custom field this understates lateness for future-dated orders.

entries.scenario: A wholesale distributor rebalances stock between its central DC and regional warehouses and wants to see how much product moved on each lane every month to plan trucking capacity.
entries.question: How many transfer orders and how many units moved between each pair of locations per month?
entries.intent: Monthly transfer-order counts and total unit quantity per from-location/to-location lane from TrnfrOrd item lines.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM') AS transfer_month,
    BUILTIN.DF(tl.location) AS from_location,
    BUILTIN.DF(t.transferlocation) AS to_location,
    COUNT(DISTINCT t.id) AS transfer_order_count,
    SUM(-tl.quantity) AS total_units_transferred
FROM transaction t
JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = t.id
JOIN item i ON i.id = tl.item
WHERE t.type = 'TrnfrOrd'
    AND tl.mainline = 'F'
    AND tl.taxline = 'F'
    AND tl.quantity < 0
    AND i.itemtype IN ('InvtPart', 'Assembly', 'Kit')
    AND t.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
    AND t.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
GROUP BY
    TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM'),
    BUILTIN.DF(tl.location),
    BUILTIN.DF(t.transferlocation)
ORDER BY
    TO_CHAR(t.trandate, 'YYYY-MM'),
    SUM(-tl.quantity) DESC
entries.caveats: TrnfrOrd item lines carry NEGATIVE quantity (outbound at the source), so quantity is negated and filtered to quantity < 0 to avoid picking up system lines. mainline='F' AND taxline='F' prevents header/tax double counting. Source location comes from transactionline.location; destination is the header field transaction.transferlocation (only populated on transfer orders). item.itemtype constrained to real stockable items so Description/other pseudo-lines are excluded. GROUP BY repeats full expressions (no aliases).

entries.scenario: An inventory planner at a multi-warehouse retail products company suspects a handful of SKUs generate most inter-warehouse shuffling and wants a ranked list to consider changing their default stocking locations.
entries.question: Which items have been transferred between locations the most in the last 6 months?
entries.intent: Top :n items ranked by total transferred unit quantity on TrnfrOrd lines over the trailing 6 months.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    i.itemid AS item_sku,
    i.displayname AS item_name,
    COUNT(DISTINCT t.id) AS transfer_order_count,
    SUM(-tl.quantity) AS total_units_transferred
FROM transaction t
JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = t.id
JOIN item i ON i.id = tl.item
WHERE t.type = 'TrnfrOrd'
    AND tl.mainline = 'F'
    AND tl.taxline = 'F'
    AND tl.quantity < 0
    AND i.itemtype IN ('InvtPart', 'Assembly', 'Kit')
    AND t.trandate >= ADD_MONTHS(TRUNC(SYSDATE, 'MM'), -6)
GROUP BY
    i.itemid,
    i.displayname
ORDER BY SUM(-tl.quantity) DESC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: Transfer-order item lines are negative, so SUM(-tl.quantity) yields positive units moved; quantity < 0 guards against non-item lines. mainline='F' AND taxline='F' required before summing lines. itemtype restricted to stockable items to exclude Subtotal/Markup/Discount pseudo-items. FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY used for the ranking (never LIMIT/OFFSET). Rolling window anchored to month start via TRUNC(SYSDATE,'MM').

entries.scenario: A supply chain manager reviews stuck inter-company and intra-company transfers weekly; transfers that sit unfulfilled tie up stock that neither location can sell.
entries.question: Show me open transfer orders with remaining unshipped quantity, oldest first, including how many days each has been open.
entries.intent: Header-level list of transfer orders with unfulfilled remaining quantity, days open, displayed status, and lane, oldest first.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    t.tranid AS transfer_number,
    t.trandate AS order_date,
    TRUNC(SYSDATE) - t.trandate AS days_open,
    BUILTIN.DF(t.status) AS status_label,
    BUILTIN.DF(t.transferlocation) AS to_location,
    MIN(BUILTIN.DF(tl.location)) AS from_location,
    MIN(BUILTIN.DF(tl.subsidiary)) AS subsidiary_name,
    SUM(-tl.quantity) AS ordered_units,
    SUM(NVL(tl.quantityshiprecv, 0)) AS shipped_units,
    SUM(-tl.quantity - NVL(tl.quantityshiprecv, 0)) AS remaining_units
FROM transaction t
JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = t.id
WHERE t.type = 'TrnfrOrd'
    AND tl.mainline = 'F'
    AND tl.taxline = 'F'
    AND tl.quantity < 0
    AND tl.isclosed = 'F'
GROUP BY
    t.tranid,
    t.trandate,
    TRUNC(SYSDATE) - t.trandate,
    BUILTIN.DF(t.status),
    BUILTIN.DF(t.transferlocation)
HAVING SUM(-tl.quantity - NVL(tl.quantityshiprecv, 0)) > 0
ORDER BY t.trandate ASC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: Openness is derived from line facts, NOT from filtering raw status codes: isclosed='F' plus HAVING remaining > 0 (ordered minus quantityshiprecv). transaction.status is displayed only via BUILTIN.DF. Line quantity is negative so ordered units = SUM(-tl.quantity); quantityshiprecv is stored positive on these lines. Subsidiary read from transactionline (transaction.subsidiary is not queryable) and wrapped in MIN() since it is constant per order. All non-aggregated SELECT expressions repeated verbatim in GROUP BY.

entries.scenario: Operations leadership at a multi-site distributor wants to know which warehouse lanes are slow to pick and ship internal transfers, to set realistic in-transit lead times in planning.
entries.question: What is the average number of days from transfer order date to fulfillment ship date, by lane?
entries.intent: Average and max days between TrnfrOrd trandate and linked ItemShip trandate per from/to location lane, using transactionline.createdfrom.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    BUILTIN.DF(x.from_loc) AS from_location,
    BUILTIN.DF(x.to_loc) AS to_location,
    COUNT(x.ful_id) AS fulfillment_count,
    ROUND(AVG(x.ship_date - x.order_date), 1) AS avg_days_order_to_ship,
    MAX(x.ship_date - x.order_date) AS max_days_order_to_ship
FROM (
    SELECT DISTINCT
        if_t.id AS ful_id,
        if_t.trandate AS ship_date,
        to_t.trandate AS order_date,
        tol.location AS from_loc,
        to_t.transferlocation AS to_loc
    FROM transaction if_t
    JOIN transactionline ifl ON ifl.transaction = if_t.id
        AND ifl.mainline = 'F'
        AND ifl.taxline = 'F'
    JOIN transaction to_t ON to_t.id = ifl.createdfrom
        AND to_t.type = 'TrnfrOrd'
    JOIN transactionline tol ON tol.transaction = to_t.id
        AND tol.mainline = 'T'
    WHERE if_t.type = 'ItemShip'
        AND if_t.trandate >= TO_DATE(:start, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
        AND if_t.trandate <= TO_DATE(:end, 'YYYY-MM-DD')
) x
GROUP BY
    BUILTIN.DF(x.from_loc),
    BUILTIN.DF(x.to_loc)
ORDER BY AVG(x.ship_date - x.order_date) DESC
entries.caveats: The fulfillment-to-order link lives on transactionline.createdfrom (not the transaction header), so the ItemShip is joined line-first then rolled up with SELECT DISTINCT to one row per fulfillment — otherwise AVG would be weighted by line count. Source location comes from the transfer order's mainline='T' header row; destination from transaction.transferlocation. Restricting createdfrom target to type='TrnfrOrd' excludes sales order fulfillments. Date subtraction returns day counts in SuiteQL's Oracle-style dialect. Partially shipped orders contribute one row per fulfillment, so multi-shipment TOs are averaged per shipment.

entries.scenario: A demand planner at a consumer goods wholesaler runs a weekly net-position check: open sales order demand against on-hand stock plus open purchase order supply, to see which SKUs will go short before inbound arrives.
entries.question: For each inventory item, compare open sales order demand vs open purchase order supply vs current on-hand, and show the projected net position, worst first.
entries.intent: Per-item comparison of open SO quantity (demand), open PO quantity (supply proxy), inventorybalance on-hand/available, and projected net position (available + open PO - open SO).
entries.sql:
SELECT
    i.itemid AS item_sku,
    i.displayname AS item_name,
    NVL(oh.qty_on_hand, 0) AS qty_on_hand,
    NVL(oh.qty_available, 0) AS qty_available,
    NVL(so.open_so_qty, 0) AS open_demand_qty,
    NVL(po.open_po_qty, 0) AS open_supply_qty,
    NVL(oh.qty_available, 0) + NVL(po.open_po_qty, 0) - NVL(so.open_so_qty, 0) AS projected_net_position
FROM item i
LEFT JOIN (
    SELECT
        ib.item AS item,
        SUM(ib.quantityonhand) AS qty_on_hand,
        SUM(ib.quantityavailable) AS qty_available
    FROM inventorybalance ib
    GROUP BY ib.item
) oh ON oh.item = i.id
LEFT JOIN (
    SELECT
        tl.item AS item,
        SUM(-tl.quantity - NVL(tl.quantityshiprecv, 0)) AS open_so_qty
    FROM transaction t
    JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = t.id
    WHERE t.type = 'SalesOrd'
        AND tl.mainline = 'F'
        AND tl.taxline = 'F'
        AND tl.isclosed = 'F'
        AND tl.quantity < 0
    GROUP BY tl.item
    HAVING SUM(-tl.quantity - NVL(tl.quantityshiprecv, 0)) > 0
) so ON so.item = i.id
LEFT JOIN (
    SELECT
        tl.item AS item,
        SUM(tl.quantity - NVL(tl.quantityshiprecv, 0)) AS open_po_qty
    FROM transaction t
    JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = t.id
    WHERE t.type = 'PurchOrd'
        AND tl.mainline = 'F'
        AND tl.taxline = 'F'
        AND tl.isclosed = 'F'
        AND tl.quantity > 0
    GROUP BY tl.item
    HAVING SUM(tl.quantity - NVL(tl.quantityshiprecv, 0)) > 0
) po ON po.item = i.id
WHERE i.itemtype = 'InvtPart'
    AND (NVL(so.open_so_qty, 0) > 0 OR NVL(po.open_po_qty, 0) > 0)
ORDER BY NVL(oh.qty_available, 0) + NVL(po.open_po_qty, 0) - NVL(so.open_so_qty, 0) ASC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
joins: transaction->transactionline (t.id = tl.transaction, SO demand inline view)
transaction->transactionline (t.id = tl.transaction, PO supply inline view)
entries.caveats: Open quantities are computed from line facts, never from status text: isclosed='F' plus (ordered - quantityshiprecv) > 0. Sales lines are NEGATIVE so demand = SUM(-tl.quantity - fulfilled); PO lines are POSITIVE so supply = SUM(quantity - received); quantityshiprecv is positive on both. inventorybalance is bin/location grain, so it is pre-aggregated per item before joining to avoid row multiplication. itemtype='InvtPart' keeps the report to stockable goods and excludes pseudo-items. Quantities span all locations and subsidiaries; net position is a company-wide proxy, not a location-level ATP.

entries.scenario: An inventory controller at a products company wants a months-of-cover view: how long current available stock will last at the recent shipping run-rate, to spot both imminent stockouts and dead stock.
entries.question: For items that shipped in the last 6 months, how many months of cover does current available inventory represent at the average monthly shipped rate?
entries.intent: Per-item coverage ratio: total quantityavailable divided by average monthly shipped quantity from ItemShip lines over the trailing 6 complete months, lowest cover first.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    i.itemid AS item_sku,
    i.displayname AS item_name,
    NVL(oh.qty_available, 0) AS qty_available,
    ROUND(sh.shipped_qty_6m / 6, 1) AS avg_monthly_shipped_qty,
    ROUND(NVL(oh.qty_available, 0) / (sh.shipped_qty_6m / 6), 1) AS months_of_cover
FROM item i
JOIN (
    SELECT
        tl.item AS item,
        SUM(-tl.quantity) AS shipped_qty_6m
    FROM transaction t
    JOIN transactionline tl ON tl.transaction = t.id
    WHERE t.type = 'ItemShip'
        AND tl.mainline = 'F'
        AND tl.taxline = 'F'
        AND tl.quantity < 0
        AND t.trandate >= ADD_MONTHS(TRUNC(SYSDATE, 'MM'), -6)
        AND t.trandate < TRUNC(SYSDATE, 'MM')
    GROUP BY tl.item
    HAVING SUM(-tl.quantity) > 0
) sh ON sh.item = i.id
LEFT JOIN (
    SELECT
        ib.item AS item,
        SUM(ib.quantityavailable) AS qty_available
    FROM inventorybalance ib
    GROUP BY ib.item
) oh ON oh.item = i.id
WHERE i.itemtype = 'InvtPart'
ORDER BY ROUND(NVL(oh.qty_available, 0) / (sh.shipped_qty_6m / 6), 1) ASC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
joins: item->transaction/transactionline (ItemShip shipped inline view, sh.item = i.id)
entries.caveats: Outbound fulfillment lines (ItemShip) are NEGATIVE, so shipped units = SUM(-tl.quantity) with quantity < 0 as a guard. The 6-month window uses complete calendar months (>= ADD_MONTHS(TRUNC(SYSDATE,'MM'),-6) AND < TRUNC(SYSDATE,'MM')) so the partial current month does not dilute the run-rate. Division by zero is impossible because the inner HAVING requires shipped > 0; items with zero recent shipments are deliberately excluded (their cover is undefined/infinite). ItemShip includes fulfillments of transfer orders as well as sales orders — the run-rate is total outbound velocity, stated as a demand proxy. inventorybalance pre-aggregated per item to collapse bin/location rows.

entries.scenario: A warehouse planner needs a daily pick-list of item/location combinations that have fallen below the reorder threshold, and wants to see whether other locations hold enough company-wide stock to justify a transfer instead of a purchase.
entries.question: Which item/location combinations are below :threshold available units, and does the company have enough stock elsewhere to transfer?
entries.intent: Item-location rows from inventorybalance where local quantityavailable is below :threshold but company-wide available exceeds it, flagging transfer-order candidates.
entries.sql:
SELECT
    i.itemid AS item_sku,
    i.displayname AS item_name,
    BUILTIN.DF(ib.location) AS location_name,
    SUM(ib.quantityonhand) AS qty_on_hand_here,
    SUM(ib.quantityavailable) AS qty_available_here,
    MAX(co.total_available) AS qty_available_companywide,
    MAX(co.total_available) - SUM(ib.quantityavailable) AS qty_available_elsewhere
FROM inventorybalance ib
JOIN item i ON i.id = ib.item
JOIN (
    SELECT
        ib2.item AS item,
        SUM(ib2.quantityavailable) AS total_available
    FROM inventorybalance ib2
    GROUP BY ib2.item
) co ON co.item = ib.item
WHERE i.itemtype = 'InvtPart'
GROUP BY
    i.itemid,
    i.displayname,
    BUILTIN.DF(ib.location)
HAVING SUM(ib.quantityavailable) < :threshold
    AND MAX(co.total_available) > :threshold
ORDER BY SUM(ib.quantityavailable) ASC
FETCH FIRST :n ROWS ONLY
entries.caveats: inventorybalance is bin-level grain, so quantities are SUMmed per item-location; the company-wide total is pre-aggregated in an inline view and read with MAX() (constant per item) to satisfy the strict GROUP BY rule. Threshold tests live in HAVING because they apply to aggregates. Location displayed via BUILTIN.DF(ib.location). The company-wide > :threshold condition filters to rows where a transfer (rather than a purchase) is plausible; a single flat :threshold is used — replace with an item reorder-point field if one is maintained. itemtype='InvtPart' excludes non-stock items.
